From the Editor: Comings and Goings

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

I wish to thank those who have given so generously of their time as Editorial Board members from January 1, 2008, through December 31, 2010. This outgoing group—Elizabeth Auchincloss, Adrienne Harris, Kenneth Levy, Eric Neutzel, Joel Whitebook, and Judith Yanof—have demonstrated their dedication, generativity, and creative engagement with authors, each contributing an enormous amount of impressive work on behalf of the journal. I hope they all found their work with JAPA intellectually and personally rewarding and that we may collaborate again in the future.

Welcome to the following new Editorial Board members, whose terms run from January 1, 2011, through December 31, 2013, though in fact they have all been hard at work since last summer: Ken Corbett, Alexandra Harrison, Jonathan House, Howard Katz, Christine C. Kieffer, and Isaac Tylim. I trust they are enjoying their work with JAPA.

I would like to thank Phyllis Tyson, whose tenure as Associate Editor for Child Analysis and Development came to a close on December 31, 2010. Phyllis's insights and scholarly contributions to this section of JAPA have been outstanding. Lee I. Ascherman succeeded her in this position on January 1. I look forward to working with him. Bonnie Litowitz has stepped down as Associate Editor for Theory and Interdisciplinary Studies, having served throughout my term as Editor and part of that of my predecessor, Arnold Richards. I have depended on Bonnie for help in innumerable contexts. She has always provided me scholarly insight and collegial support and has assisted a host of authors in bringing their contributions to the high level of scholarship and prose that JAPA is known for. I will miss her greatly. Ellen Rees assumes Bonnie's duties, beginning her term as Associate Editor on March 1.

Just to keep you up to date on a few of JAPA's recent initiatives:

JAPA Psa-Netcast & JAPA Podcasts. The Netcast is on hiatus until we identify a new Internet Editor. Meanwhile we are working with Sage to develop a series of podcasts. These will consist of interviews with authors of recent papers, who will discuss in greater depth the issues their papers raise. The first podcast to appear will feature Mari Ruti, whose exploration of singularity was published in JAPA 58/6.

[[RunningHead]]

Response to Commentaries

John Kafka

John Kafka

7834 Aberdeen Road
Bethesda, MD 20814
E-mail: jkafka@tidalwave.net

John Kafka  

I want to express my gratitude to the commentators. Because of the richness of their texts, I must limit my responses to a few central points. Some similar questions are raised by two of the commentators, and one commentator raises very different questions.

A few of the reactions to the picture of Chestnut Lodge that I have presented have made me aware that, because of my emphasis on intensive individual therapy, I may not have sufficiently described other aspects of the work there, such as Stanton and Schwartz's therapeutic community approach, Marian Chase's pioneering movement and dance therapy, innovative art therapy, occupational therapy (which included patients’ running their own shop), various levels of group therapy, the nature of the weekly all-hospital meeting in which patients raised questions of self-determination and suggested programs and activities, and a uniquely designed family program in which families stayed for a week at the Frieda Fromm-Reichmann cottage on the grounds. During these visits, intra- and extrafamilial interactions were observed. Families were given feedback, and the observations were discussed with the patients separately and in various family groupings. I have reported findings from these family programs in a paper coauthored with J.S. McDonald (Kafka and McDonald 1964). Some of the work went beyond traditional approaches. Together with the social worker and the chronically hospitalized patient, for instance, we visited the home in which the patient had grown up and in which the family still lived. Without this visit, we could never have guessed how bizarre were, and had always been, the family's sleeping arrangements, information useful in the therapy of the patient and the family.

While some patients were hospitalized for long periods, there was also an adjacent halfway house. Partial hospitalizations were sometimes arranged. Most important, however, discharged patients continued their therapy with the therapist who had treated them at the Lodge whenever such continuity was possible. Concerning the use of medication, much of what I have described at the Lodge occurred before the introduction of modern psychopharmacology. When that became available, however, it was used, and the interactions of medication with intensive therapy began to be studied.

[[RunningHead]]

Journal Watch

Ruud Custers & Henk Aarts (2010). The unconscious will: How the pursuit of goals operates outside of conscious awareness.Science329:47-50.

Steven Roose

Steven Roose

Steven Roose  

Perhaps no postulate is dearer to those embracing the psychoanalytic model of the mind than the dynamic unconscious. Though Freud's complex theory of the unconscious did not lend itself to experimental testing, recent research in the behaviorist tradition has produced results quite compatible with a modern psychoanalytic concept of the dynamic unconscious. In a comprehensive yet pithy review article in Science, Custers and Aarts discuss research challenging the concept that conscious decisions are what determine behavior. The research shows that “goals themselves can arise and operate unconsciously. Social situations and stimuli in the surroundings activate or prime goals in people's minds outside of their awareness, thereby motivating and guiding them. … Thus goals and their pursuit can be influenced by unconscious sources, and these goals do not need to be consciously set and adopted before their influence begins to operate.” In fact, the authors contend that only if we assume that people have unconscious goals does much of human behavior become understandable.

Goals are often pursued without conscious awareness of their presence and therefore are a source of motivated behavior. Even if there is a conscious perception of a goal, the authors contend that “the value or in other words, the reward, that the attainment of a goal carries is a critical factor when the unconscious mind assigns a priority to a goal.” Neuroimaging studies have shown that one of the critical variables that determine the priority of a goal is how reward cues are processed by limbic structures. We can infer that this means that high-priority goals have emotional, not simply executive, importance to the individual. The reward centers respond not only to stimuli linked to hardwired survival functions, but also to learned stimuli that have a positive valence, such as praise.

In summary, the experimental evidence supports the paradigm-shifting argument that goals direct attention and behavior, even when the individual has no conscious awareness of the goal: “that is, the operation of higher cognitive processes, supporting goal pursuit (also conceptualized as working memory or executive control) does not care much about the conscious state of the individual.

[[RunningHead]]

L. Pessoa & R. Adolphs (2010). Emotion processing and the amygdala: From a “low road” to “many roads” of evaluating biological significance.Nature Reviews Neuroscience11:773-782.

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi  

Emotions are central in psychoanalysis and take an important place both in our theories and in clinical practice. For over a century, psychoanalysts have understood that emotions, even unconscious ones, can be highly complex states. Affective neuroscience has indeed confirmed that affect, to some extent, can be processed outside of awareness. However, an implicit assumption has been that the processing of these emotions is fairly automatic, fast, and somewhat coarse. Fine-grained details are subsequently elaborated in the cortex.

In a model known as the standard hypothesis, this fast and automatic type of emotional processing relies on a subcortical pathway converging onto the amygdala, an almond-shaped collection of nuclei located in the temporal lobe and long thought to play a central role in a variety of affect-related processes. This subcortical route, known as the low road, carries emotionally salient visual information from the superior colliculi (which control eye movements based on direct inputs from the retina) through the pulvinar (part of the thalamus) and into the amygdala.

Announcing the JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA is pleased to announce a new annual prize to recognize significant contributions to the journal by new authors. The New Author Prize will be awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and will include a $500 honorarium to be presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author will be considered someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. The editor will establish a mentoring/editorial group to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the usual JAPA submission process using the online system accessed at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, an author may indicate in the cover letter that the manuscript is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that the paper be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication will remain with the editorial board and the editor-in-chief, as will the decision about selecting the annual prize-winning paper.

Beginning to Grow: Five Studies. By Sylvia Brody. New York: International Psychoanalytic Books, 2009, 222 pp., $27.50.

Barry Landau

Barry Landau

5410 Connecticut Avenue NW
Washington, DC 20015
E-mail: barryjlandau@verizon.net

Barry Landau  

It is not often that one gets to read a book discussing psychoanalytic research that studies the development of individuals from birth through eighteen years of age and beyond. This alone would make Sylvia Brody's Beginning to Grow: Five Studies an interesting and valuable one. Brody is a psychoanalytic researcher who has published a number of books and articles on longitudinal developmental studies focusing on the impact of the child's early experiences, especially in interaction with parents, on the emergence of character structure. This fact only adds to the interest one experiences in approaching this book.

The centerpiece of the present book is a detailed summary of the findings of Brody's research on five of the 131 children who were the subjects of her earlier studies.

There are two main themes, the first announced in the opening words of the preface: “One day when I was ten … I came upon an article entitled, ‘Children Are People.’ I was astonished that someone else in the big world outside also realized that. By the time I was twelve, I was sure that if I were ever in a position to do anything about it, I would establish a law that no one could have a child without a license.

El Narcisismo y El Trabajo del Analista: Paradojas, Obstáculos y Transformaciones (Narcissism and the Analyst's Task: Paradoxes, Obstacles, Transformations). By Jorge Maldonado. Buenos Aires: Lumen, Colecciön de Psicología Interpretativa, Perspectivista e Interdisciplinaria, 2008, 271 pp., $25.00.

Irene Cairo

Irene Cairo

5 West 86th Street
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: irecai@aol.com

Irene Cairo  

Jorge Maldonado's eminently clinical collection of essays is a readable, thoughtful, quite original work. Some of Maldonado's papers have been published in English in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, so readers may already be familiar with some of his ideas (1984, 1987, 1989, 1993, 1999, 2003, 2005).

Maldonado belongs to a generation of Argentine psychoanalysts who, having been immersed in Freud, Klein, and the post-Kleinians, opened themselves to influences from a wide spectrum of French authors beyond Lacan, such as Didier Anzieu and André Green. Thus, his exquisite attunement to clinical problems comes from a varied and profound theoretical background. As Horacio Etchegoyen notes in a warm and laudatory prologue, Maldonado approaches the problems narcissistic patients pose by emphasizing that the greatest difficulty is their manifest lack of interest in investigating their psychic reality. The world of representation is inaccessible to them, or meaningless. They live in a world of action; their curiosity, only apparently extinguished, is transformed into voyeurism focused on the analyst and the analyst's task. The narcissistic patient seems impermeable to knowledge.

As Maldonado points out, psychic reality emerges from the tolerance of the object's absence, which then permits creation of the symbol. Further, Maldonado stresses that beyond Freud's delineation of the opposition love/hate (1915), there is indifference, the hallmark of narcissistic personalities. However, in a highly original formulation, Maldonado maintains that the narcissistic personality desperately needs the object, the other who will show he is affected by the contempt implied in the indifference.

The book is divided into three sections: Theoretical Aspects; Narcissism in Clinical Experience; and Disturbances in the Analytic Process. There is in addition an appendix, Problems regarding Mourning and Trauma.

In the first part, Maldonado addresses the general characteristics and varieties of narcissistic pathology. As noted above, a characteristic common to these patients is a lack of curiosity about their internal world, their interest having been displaced to a voyeurism centered on the analyst's mental activity.

[[RunningHead]]

La tentation psychotique [the psychotic temptation]. By Liliane Abensour. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, 2008, 208 pp., €12.00.

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: lewis_kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner  

In this slim but densely reasoned book, Liliane Abensour weaves an intricate fabric, considering the possibility and necessity for creative transformation of the self, drawing from her clinical experience with psychotic patients at the Kestemberg Center in Paris and her own psychoanalytic practice. Her wide-ranging sources include theories of psychosis from Freud, Winnicott, and many French contributors, as well as literary figures who have attempted to depict the process of subjective creation.

Abensour bases her argument for creative self-fashioning on the notion of a problematic initial inscription of the psychotic subject in the infantile world of the drives, as proposed by Freud. With emphasis on the fate of the sexual and aggressive impulses and their constant remodeling in the après-coup, Freud's theory takes into account the constant to-and-fro of temporal movement between past and present. Abensour makes a frank distinction between the modes of neurotic and psychotic mental functioning in relation to this temporal movement. Her interest is in their different ways of using space and time to anchor the subject, with the psychotic living in a perpetual present that cannot enrich itself from an unconscious or infantile past, while simultaneously facing a frightening external reality.

Because the psychotic lacks attachment to a secure base of primary experiences that have established a functioning self, he is unlikely to benefit from a therapeutic attempt analogous to the treatment of neurotics. The exploration of developmental origins, Abensour argues, can engulf and explode the fragile ego of the psychotic by reviving a traumatic overstimulation or its counterpart, the terrifying void of total absence. In this situation, the “psychotic temptation” of imposing barriers both to the unconscious and to external reality has been the sole recourse for survival for many patients and indicates their extreme fragility. The analyst's role is therefore not to interpret or explore possible links, but to engage the patient in the domain of the actual in which he lives.

Dave Eggers's A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius: Memoir as a “Pain-Relief Device”

Elise Miller

Elise Miller

625 Talbot Avenue
Albany, CA 94706
E-mail: emiller@stmarys-ca.edu

Elise Miller   Lecturer, School of Liberal Arts, Saint Mary's College of California; Instructor, University of California, Berkeley, Fall Program for Freshmen.

Dave Eggers's memoir is an important addition to the tradition of autobiography in America, and offers significant contributions to our understanding of creativity, sublimation, and the psychology of the memoir-writing process. A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius is really two books—an autobiographical narrative about unbearable suffering, and a book of psychoanalytic commentary on the challenges of writing a memoir about catastrophic loss and trauma. The main narrative suggests the psychological resilience it takes to contain unbearable suffering. The metanarrative renders transparent the mind of someone who is both remembering his losses and constantly reflecting upon the process of writing about them. Eggers's identification with authorship, rather than bereavement or traumatization, may have played an important role in containing his affect and his sense of self when the heartbreaking events were originally unfolding. But a price is paid when a son uses his art to construct a new identity as an author—unconscious conflicts, primitive affect, anxieties about failing, as well as guilt about succeeding—consequences often missed by readers. Heartbreaking is a palimpsest, a story about story-telling superimposed on tales of death and survival, but its messages will be missed unless all its parts are preserved when being read or studied.

[[RunningHead]]

Presenting Problems: The Short Films of Garrick Duckler, Vol. 1. Films by Garrick Duckler. DVD. Toronto: Caversham Productions, 2011, 30 minutes, $20.00.

David Sterritt

David Sterritt

1 East Chase Street #501-502
Baltimore, MD 21202-2574
E-mail: djsterritt@gmail.com

David Sterritt  

Brevity may be the soul of wit, as Polonius says in Hamlet, but it is not the soul of psychoanalysis, which is famously generous in the time it allots to probing memories, fantasies, and associations. Brevity is also scarce in the movie world these days, when even Harry Potter pictures feel obliged to clock in at marathon lengths once reserved for major literary and historical productions.

The four movies in Presenting Problems: The Short Films of Garrick Duckler, Vol. 1 are therefore something of a surprise. They deal with psychoanalytic subjects, but they do so with a concision that could teach a trick or two to Hollywood, and maybe to clinicians as well. The total running time of Duckler's collected works to date is about half an hour, and that includes The Grudge, a veritable epic at eleven minutes long. All are exercises in vernacular psychology, using impressionistic imagery and elliptical, allegorical stories to pose questions about psychological conflict without laying claim to easy answers.

It isn't clear from watching the films whether their main audience is meant to be psychotherapists, patients and clients, interested outsiders, or all of the above. My first impression was that Duckler made them primarily for young people who need a gentle, nonjudgmental nudge toward exploring their emotions, and who quickly feel at home with films that don't quite fit traditional categories—these aren't Hollywood-style animations, but they're painted, drawn, or collaged rather than shot with movie cameras. The films are not simplistic morality lessons, though, and they move at a spirited pace requiring a reasonably healthy attention span. Adolescents should find them stimulating, and open-minded adults won't feel Duckler is speaking down to them.

In the spirit of Duckler's admirable pithiness, I'll give just a brief idea of the films' content. An unseen narrator (presumably Duckler himself) tells the stories, which are illustrated, punctuated, and enlivened by the on-screen artwork. At the Movies uses vintage motion-picture stills to present a rapid-fire look at the perils and pleasures of fantasy. The Monster within: The Hidden Side of Motherhood. By Barbara Almond. Berkeley: University of California Press, 2010, 291 pp., $27.50 hardcover, $19.95 paperback.

Barbara Stimmel

Barbara Stimmel

1185 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: barbara.stimmel@mssm.edu

Barbara Stimmel  

Barbara Almond, a practicing psychoanalyst, has written an interesting and informative book about a hitherto forbidden topic. Maternal ambivalence is an inescapable part of the human experience. It is set into place most likely when the first thoughts of pregnancy arise, through conception, gestation, childbirth, and the life of the child. And even before, it must be part of the little girl's fantasied child, when playing with dolls or dreaming of Daddy's baby.

Maternal ambivalence does not disappear, ever. This is less of a problem than are the denial and negation of these ordinary, complicated feelings. And sometimes, in part because of their rejection by the mother herself and those around her, these emotions rise to another level of meaning and action; Andrea Yates could very well be the result.

Almond leads the reader through a skilled narration of the many complex scenarios with which mothers, would-be mothers, and never-to-be mothers are confronted as they make their decisions and work their way through the maze of motherhood. Her book is essentially a primer of psychoanalytic thought and language. Almond exhibits deft skill in presenting her convictions in plain language with no watering down of her ideas. She is intent on introducing readers to a world of thought presumably outside their usual discourse.

We encounter patients, characters in literature, movie stars, friends, and family as Almond invites her readers to consider with her the whys and wherefores of the experience of mothering. Her most informative contribution to the lay reader lies in her ability to describe, through example and analogy, the painful and confusing experience of ambivalence, with its hydra-headed complexity. She also educates the reader about the vagaries of projection, identification, projective identification, and denial. By taking these topics seriously and at the same time respecting her readers, Almond has written essentially a textbook for the lay audience. In expecting them to be able, and willing, to follow her, she has created a valuable, hopefully eye-opening and mind-expanding opportunity for increased self-knowledge.

Throughout his career, Freud reevaluated and defended the core psychoanalytic concept of the unconscious. In a paper from 1915, he insists “that our assumption of the unconscious is necessary and legitimate” (Freud 1915, p. 166). Necessary, because consciousness alone does not supply sufficient data to illuminate all cognitive and affective workings of the mind; legitimate, because we are justified in inferring that commonly accepted limits of fully knowing the external, physical world correspond to similar limitations of consciously knowing the internal, psychical world. Hence, the pursuit of psychoanalysis as a science of the mind's unconscious processes is as legitimate as Kant's critical exploration of the capacities of perception and reason: “Just as Kant warned us not to overlook the fact that our perceptions are subjectively conditioned and must not be regarded as identical with what is perceived though unknowable, so psycho-analysis warns us not to equate perceptions by means of consciousness with the unconscious mental processes which are their object” (p. 171).

Announcing the JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA is pleased to announce a new annual prize to recognize significant contributions to the journal by new authors. The New Author Prize will be awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and will include a $500 honorarium to be presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author will be considered someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. The editor will establish a mentoring/editorial group to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the usual JAPA submission process using the online system accessed at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, an author may indicate in the cover letter that the manuscript is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that the paper be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication will remain with the editorial board and the editor-in-chief, as will the decision about selecting the annual prize-winning paper.

Introduction to Weinberger and Smith

Andrew Gerber

Andrew Gerber

E-mail: gerbera@childpsych.columbia.edu

Andrew Gerber  

For anyone who reads the cognitive neuroscience literature or even gets a glimpse at the raft of recent New York Times articles and popular science books on this subject, it is increasingly obvious that the idea of the unconscious mind is as mainstream now in psychology and cognitive science as it has ever been. John Kihlstrom's 1987 article “The Cognitive Unconscious,” appearing in Science, the most mainstream of American professional science journals, led the way for an ever increasing number of empirical studies over the next decades. Collectively they have shown that unconscious processing (a) influences memory and perception (Fisk and Haase in press), (b) influences motivation (Custers and Aarts 2010), (c) involves emotion (Tamietto and de Gelder 2010), and (d) has the complexity to involve multiple simultaneous and competing sources (Lau and Passingham 2007). Of course the content of these findings comes as no surprise to psychoanalytic clinicians and researchers. However, the fact that the fields of psychology and cognitive science, so often thought of these days as hostile to and ignorant of psychoanalysis, are doing these experiments and considering them important may surprise some who had given up hope.

What then remains for cognitive science and psychoanalytic thinking to collaborate on next? A lot, as it turns out. While research on the unconscious proliferates, it remains a challenging task to design studies that might change the practice of even experienced clinicians. The all-too-common refrain from psychoanalysts reading the cognitive science literature is, “Well, it's nice that they're now doing that, but we're known that for decades!” Or even more worrisome, a cursory reading of the literature might lead some to conclude that it is unlikely or even impossible that experiments of the kind cognitive science designs tell us anything sufficiently nuanced and subtle about the unconscious to be beyond what we already know as psychoanalytic theorists and clinicians (Hoffman 2009; Blass and Carmeli 2007).

Psychoanalyisis in the U.S.: Recovered Memories and New Experiences of a Former Editor

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: tshapiro@med.Cornell.edu

Theodore Shapiro   Professor Emeritus, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Psychoanalysis in the first decade of the twenty-first century seems a very different discipline than it was during my tenure as editor of JAPA (1984-1993). As a former editor, I will take this opportunity to examine the scene today in contrast to what was and thereby mark our current boundaries by comparison. I will describe what I experienced at the end of my tenure and then review the ten-year period of that tenure, referring to some of the issues that seemed important then, before moving to the present.

I wrote somewhat coyly in my final editorial, “A View from the Bridge” (Shapiro 1993), “Now there is no such thing as an analysand without an analyst” (p. 926). This paraphrase of Winnicott suggestively indicates what I then thought was the change in focus in psychoanalysis at the end of my term. I specifically did not say whether it was healthy for psychoanalysis or a regression to a less formed, even amorphous, infantile state. As an editor, I was viewing the transformations I had witnessed in a brief ten years from the earlier experiences of the first editor, John Frosch, and his successor, Harold Blum. I also noted in that final statement that self-observation had become as central as patient observation and that a two-person psychology and subjectivity seemed inevitable for future theories.

This strongly subjective point of view was challenged by a cautionary exhortation from the Committee on Scientific Affairs (cited in Shapiro 1993). They wrote that the traditional case reports and cases as exemplars of theoretical principles could not serve as data for any scientific compilation or metaanalysis that psychoanalysts might wish to use to demonstrate a proposition. The cases presented had been disguised and focused to make a point and thus were biased. Such warnings seemed irrelevant to the writing psychoanalyst of my time. In addition, hermeneutics had taken hold.

Steven Roose

Steven Roose

Steven Roose  

When reading the psychodynamic psychotherapy and psychoanalytic research literature, it is hard to ignore the sometimes contentious and even disrespectful schism between investigators primarily interested in psychoanalytic process and those primarily interested in outcome. Outcome researchers lament that if process research does not include measures of outcome, we have no way of evaluating whether process is connected to therapeutic gain. Process researchers lament that outcome researchers, interested only in the study endpoint and not at all in understanding the mechanism of action of the treatments, cannot illuminate what is therapeutic about the psychoanalytic situation.

In “Therapeutic Interventions Related to Outcome in Psychodynamic Psychotherapy for Anxiety Disorder Patients,” Slavin-Mulford et al. report a robust association between certain techniques and therapeutic outcome in patients with anxiety disorders treated with psychodynamic psychotherapy. In this study, twenty-one patients were treated with once- or twice-weekly sessions in short-term psychodynamic psychotherapy; the mean number of sessions was twenty-four. The key features of the short-term psychodynamic psychotherapy model include “(1) Focus on affect and the expression of emotion; (2) The identification of patterns in actions, thoughts, feelings, experiences, and relationships with these patterns being explored/formulated using the core conflictual relationship theme format; (3) Emphasis on past experiences; (4) Focus on interpersonal experiences; (5) Emphasis on the therapeutic relationship/alliance; (6) Exploration of wishes, dreams, or fantasies; and (7) Exploration of attempts to avoid topics or engage in activities that may hinder the progress of therapy. In addition to these areas of treatment focus, case presentations and symptoms are conceptualized in the context of interpersonal/intrapsychic conflict.

From the Editor: Our First Century

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

This year marks the hundredth anniversary of the American Psychoanalytic Association. JAPA, the association's scholarly journal, founded in 1953, is joining in celebrating this milestone in the organization's history by publishing, in the 2011 volume, two series of celebratory articles. The first consists of articles examining contributions of American psychoanalysis that particularly reflect our national, cultural, and intellectual context. Second, I have commissioned from each of the four living JAPA editors, past and present, a piece addressing our experiences during the decade of our tenure.

Organizing the first series required difficult choices, given the breadth and depth of subjects and perspectives engaged in by American psychoanalysts during the hundred-year history of our organization. I have aimed to highlight representative themes rather than for comprehensiveness, in each instance looking for lines of clinical and/or theoretical inquiry that reflect characteristically “American” perspectives and interests. The first of these articles comes from John Kafka. Appearing in this issue, it considers the role played by “psychoanalytic” hospitals in the development of American psychoanalysis. These institutions served as training and intellectual centers focused on understanding primitive mental functioning, manifest in psychotic illness but seen also in borderline and other severe psychopathology. Chestnut Lodge, Austen Riggs, Sheppard Pratt, the Menninger Clinic, and several other institutions should not be underestimated as generative centers for psychoanalytic thought, practice, and education. It is a theme touched on again in Arnold Richards's piece on his years as editor of JAPA; he was profoundly influenced by his training at one such institution.

The other articles will appear later in the year. Lawrence Friedman addresses the work of Charles Brenner, a quintessentially American psychoanalyst.

Bret Rutherford  

Since Leo Stone's discussion of the “widening scope of indications for psychoanalysis,” the effects of treating more patients with severe personality disorders and other psychiatric comorbidities with psychoanalysis have been debated. Until research studies specific to psychoanalysis shed light on this issue, one must turn to the broader psychiatric literature for data bearing on this topic. A relevant article was recently published in the Archives of General Psychiatry by a broad consortium of experts in Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD). Of note, the disorder referenced in the following discussion is BPD as defined by DSM-IV, not the different concept of Borderline Personality Organization used by analysts. John Gunderson and colleagues have conducted a multisite longitudinal study of the course of BPD and its associated functional limitations called the Collaborative Longitudinal Personality Disorders Study (CLPS). The 175 patients diagnosed with BPD enrolled in the study initially presented to inpatient and outpatient treatment settings and then consented to be assessed by the investigators at baseline, 6 months, 12 months, and 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 years later. Presence of the diagnosis of BPD was evaluated by means of structured diagnostic interviews at each time point, and the Longitudinal Interval Follow-up Evaluation (LIFE) was used to assess functioning over time. LIFE includes subscale scores for measuring functional impairment in relationships, recreation, employment, and satisfaction.

Strikingly, Gunderson et al. found that 85 percent of patients diagnosed with BPD experienced remission (meeting two or fewer diagnostic criteria for at least twelve months) over the ten-year follow-up period.

Marie Rudden  

In this volume, Werner Bohleber offers one of the most thoughtful and comprehensive discussions I have read of modern developments in our field. The book is an essential addition to the library of any psychoanalyst who seeks to understand new insights into our field and to place them in context in a fair, coherent, and intelligent manner.

The book, translated from German by four different scholars, is divided into three sections that reflect Bohleber's primary research interests: The Intersubjective Paradigm in Psychoanalysis and Late Modernity; Trauma, Memory, and Historical Context; and Psychoanalysis of Ideological Destructivity. Each section is filled with a wealth of detail about the philosophical and psychoanalytic developments that have contributed to the author's evolving viewpoint.

The first section offers a lucid introduction and analysis of intersubjective theories, which emphasize the reciprocal influence between patient and analyst. Bohleber appreciates the intention of these theories to develop concepts about the unconscious interactions and enactments that occur in the analytic dyad. He particularly values the intersubjectivists' effort to capture crucial aspects of the experience of analytic interaction not articulated in the concept of transference: “Concepts of encounter and the relation of the self to the Other allow for the introduction of an excess of meaning into a theory of the therapeutic relationship, something that cannot be subsumed by the analysis of transference and countertransference, but which only emerges as present experience, and which must then be caught up with verbally, though the attempt can never fully succeed” (pp. 21-22). Bohleber contends that this new emphasis must, however, be conceptually placed alongside the separate but still crucial analysis of transference and countertransference. How to balance this dual focus technically is not addressed; one wishes that it had been, with clinical examples.

Paula Bernstein  

Psychoanalysis and Male Homosexuality: Twentieth Anniversary Edition by Kenneth Lewes gave me the opportunity to read this oft-cited classic for the first time. Lewes's scholarly review exposed the homophobic bias that permeated psychoanalytic writings on homosexuality. Two books, newly published in 2009, speak to significant changes in psychoanalytic ideas about male homosexuality since Lewes first spoke out. Mark Blechner's Sex Changes tells of a shift in the attitudes of society and psychoanalysis toward homosexuality from the perspective of a gay psychoanalyst who lived through the changes. The papers collected by Bruce Reis and Robert Grossmark in Heterosexual Masculinities show how feminist and queer theory have impacted contemporary views of the psychology of heterosexual men, recalibrating the discourse in the psychoanalytic literature about the “feminine” aspects of man's psyche.

Lewes's review—starting with Freud and ending in 1982—is devastating to read, because of the problems it reveals about the integrity of the psychoanalytic method itself. In his preface to this new edition, Donald Moss writes of this “life/death embrace” between psychoanalysis and male homosexuality: “The two seem bound no less intensively than, say, the police and black people are bound in every urban American center” (p. xix). And yet Lewes still holds out the hope that genuine psychoanalytic inquiry can be fruitful and helpful.

Opening Doors: Reflections of a Former JAPA Editor

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street #45B
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: arniedr.@aol.com

Arnold Richards  

Let the voices multiply; the more voices we have, the more truth will finally emerge.

—Wayne C. Booth (1979, p. 4)

I am honored and pleased to have been invited by Steve Levy to write about my experience as editor of JAPA from 1994 to 2003. When I took office, I knew that I had large shoes to fill. The editors of JAPA over its first forty years (John Frosch, Harold Blum, and Ted Shapiro) had made it the premier psychoanalytic journal in United States. But when I took office, the psychoanalytic landscape—organizational, political, and clinical—was a very different place than it had been in their time.

JAPA was founded in 1953. Then, and for many years after, its parent organization, APsaA, was essentially the only game on this side of the Atlantic. There were other psychoanalytic organizations in this country and other avenues to psychoanalytic training. But APsaA had all the advantages in age, size, and weight. APsaA was Freud's heir; he still loomed over it heavily in the persons of the emigré analysts who had been close to him in Europe. Circumscribed by the insistence of its founder, A. A. Brill, on developing psychoanalysis as a medical specialty, APsaA held tight to a sharply focused vision of psychoanalysis, the only one (as APsaA saw it) faithful to its origins. These were fat years at APsaA. There were plenty of patients and plenty of candidates. Psychoanalysis was respected almost to the point of adulation in some quarters, and the psychoanalytic worldview did not confine itself to the clinical. It was influential in the academy, and was explored with excitement in popular books and movies as well. The APsaA of the fifties and sixties was a theoretically narrow but very powerful organization, and its immensely prestigious journal was the natural stage for its most powerful members.

In the decade of Harold Blum's editorship (1974-1983), American-trained analysts came into new prominence and the topographic model gave way to the structural model.

Prudence Gourguechon

Prudence Gourguechon

1454 West Farragut Avenue
Chicago, IL 60640
E-mail: prudygourguechon@gmail.com

Prudence Gourguechon  

My address today is titled “The Citizen Psychoanalyst.” I want to talk to you about psychoanalytic advocacy and psychoanalytic social commentary, aspects of what is known as applied analysis.

I hope to make a convincing argument that engaging in these two activities is an important adventure for psychoanalysts. I offer two arguments. First, that psychoanalytic commentary and advocacy inculcate in the public mind the idea that there is a story beneath the surface of all human phenomena. That there is an inferiority to human activities. These, as you all know, are endangered concepts in our culture today. And second, it is only by showing our face in the public sphere, in the “commons,” that we can be known, and therefore survive as a profession with the necessary flow of new ideas, new patients, and new trainees into our field.

I am using the term psychoanalytic advocacy to refer to a psychoanalyst's or psychoanalytic organization's taking a principled stand on a public issue and attempting to push the resolution of that issue in a particular direction. With the phrase psychoanalytic social commentary I am referring to the psychoanalyst's offering public comment on a matter already before the public eye. Here the analyst does not hold a particular agenda, other than advancing psychoanalysis and our way of thinking.

I am convinced that many human beings, not just psychoanalysts, are bound and determined to explain the phenomena around them, especially those that confound them.

I have also noticed that attempts to find explanations for what underlies disturbing events come into the public mind very quickly following an event.

Plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 15, 2011.

Editors' Note: The following review was scheduled to be one in an occasional series of essays on influential psychoanalytic books not available in English. As the essay was approaching completion, so too was an English translation of the book. Under the editorship of Leo Bleger and John Churcher, the translation is a collaboration of the editors with Susan Rogers. There will be a preface by Horacio Etchegoyen and another by the editors. Tentatively titled Symbiosis and Ambiguity, the translation will be published by Routledge in the New Library of Psychoanalysis and can be expected in late 2011. An earlier English edition was commissioned in 1987 by Free Association Books, and for many years this translation was advertised as forthcoming, under the title Symbiosis and Ambiguity: The Psychoanalysis of Very Early Experience. A draft translation was made, but the translator became terminally ill while working on it. The agreement between the Blegers, who own the copyright, and Free Association Books expired in 1989. Although that version was never completed or published, references to it as a forthcoming title by Process Press (successors to Free Association) can still be found on the internet (e.g., on the Amazon and Karnac websites), complete with an ISBN number and publication dates of 2001 or 2002.

South American psychoanalysis, particularly in Argentina, has a character of its own, whether a practitioner sees himself as Kleinian, Lacanian, or more traditionally Freudian. The early psychoanalysts arrived in Argentina before World War II from a variety of European countries, with a consequent variety of psychoanalytic training and theoretical backgrounds. From Celes Carcamo, who trained in Paris, and Maria Langer, trained in Vienna, to Angel Garma, who trained in Berlin, the result was the development of a unique, highly original, heterogeneous brand of clinical and theoretical thinking that only now is being recognized internationally for its many early contributions.

Mixed Methods Research Design for Pragmatic Psychoanalytic Studies

Jane Tillman , A. Clemence and Jennifer Stevens

Jennifer Stevens

25 Main Street
P.O. Box 962
Stockbridge, MA 01262.
E-mail: jane.tillman@austenriggs.net

Jennifer Stevens   Clinical psychologist and psychoanalyst, Austen Riggs Center.

Calls for more rigorous psychoanalytic studies have increased over the past decade. The field has been divided by those who assert that psychoanalysis is properly a hermeneutic endeavor and those who see it as a science. A comparable debate is found in research methodology, where qualitative and quantitative methods have often been seen as occupying orthogonal positions. Recently, Mixed Methods Research (MMR) has emerged as a viable “third community” of research, pursuing a pragmatic approach to research endeavors through integrating qualitative and quantitative procedures in a single study design. Mixed Methods Research designs and the terminology associated with this emerging approach are explained, after which the methodology is explored as a potential integrative approach to a psychoanalytic human science. Both qualitative and quantitative research methods are reviewed, as well as how they may be used in Mixed Methods Research to study complex human phenomena.

To date, no literature connects psychoanalytic ideas on relational dynamics to an adolescent refugee population. Further, although psychoanalysis was founded by Sigmund Freud, himself to become a refugee, only a few articles link psychoanalytic ideas to refugee populations (Akhtar 1995; Alayarian 2007; Clark and Garner 2005; Volkan 2003). As Alayarian (2007) acknowledges, relationships are central to psychoanalysis, and many refugees have lost all their relational ties. Grounded in psychoanalytic thought, attachment theory provides a lens for viewing refugee relationships by conceptualizing the effects of relational loss and trauma. This exploratory study sought to answer the question, How do adolescent refugees' attachment styles influence their experiences of traumatic events?

In 2009, 60, 191 refugees were admitted to the United States (U.S. Committee for Refugees and Immigrants 2010). Estimates suggest that children account for at least half that number (Wasem 2004). Many refugees enter this country bearing mental and physical problems resulting from war. Indeed, research maintains that nearly half of all young refugees experience trauma-related mental health problems (Ehntholt and Yule 2006). Yet certain protective factors may mitigate these potentially adverse effects. One such factor is security of attachment (Arnold 2006; Rousseau, Mekki-Berrada, and Moreau 2001; Rousseau, Drapeau, and Rahimi 2003).

Stanley Leavy

Stanley Leavy

200 Leader Hill Drive
Hamden, CT 06517

Stanley Leavy  

We can't know whether Sigmund Freud would have said anything at all about the psychology of mysticism had the novelist Romain Rolland not written him a letter in which he asked Freud's opinion about what he called the “oceanic feeling.” Freud's comments suggest that the subject was not unknown to him, but that it was an uneasy one for him. To feel in immediate touch with the external world, identify subjectively with it, the screen between perception and perceiver absent, or to report periods in time without awareness of transience—these carried a note of the uncanny, which Freud attributed to defensive incursions of the unconscious on present consciousness, with a return to the undifferentiated unity of mother and infant. Nor was the frequent alliance of such experiences with religious claims likely to make them welcome to analysis other than as symptoms to be diagnosed. The atmosphere of radical positivism that psychoanalysis breathed from its beginnings did not encourage recognition of unitive experience as part of the wealth of what it means to be human—as art, for example, was treated. Ana-Maria Rizzuto (1998), in her illuminating study of Freud's atheism, quotes his revealing and typically honest words to Rolland: “To me mysticism is just as closed a book as music” (citing Freud's letter to Rolland [Freud 1960]).

Dan Merkur, a Toronto psychoanalyst and noted scholar in the psychology of mysticism, in effect illustrates in this book some of the ways that analysts more sympathetic than Freud to unitive experience have accounted for it. Merkur's painstaking summaries of the theoretical developments on which these analysts rely show in each instance the structures, some innovative, some not, on which they severally understand mystical experience. If not an exhaustive review of their overall theoretical contributions, the book is a good introduction to them.

Jeanne Harasemovitch   Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; lecturer, Oregon Psychoanalytic Center and the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; editorial board member, fort da.

The intimate relationship between timelessness, time, and transference is generative in its clinical impact on analysis. A tendency to define and represent timelessness and time as opposing structures, antithetical in aim, can obscure the fertility and complexity of their relationship. The creative and transformative power of an (a)temporal dialectic is proposed whose engagement with transference destroys and creates new paradigms of human experience, presenting analyst and analysand with fresh ways of thinking and being. A brief history of timelessness and time in psychoanalysis follows their intertwined developmental path and their import in transference-countertransference experience.

[[RunningHead]]

Ernst Kris: The Objects of Memory

Nellie Thompson

Nellie Thompson

520 West 114th Street #21
New York, NY 10025-7815
E-mail: Nelliet100@aol.com

Nellie Thompson   Historian and member, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; curator, Archives and Special Collections, the A. A. Brill Library of the New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; board member, Sigmund Freud Archives.

In 1956, the year before his death, Ernst Kris published a trilogy of papers on memory—“The Personal Myth,” “On Some Vicissitudes of Insight in Psychoanalysis,” and “The Recovery of Childhood Memories”—and was planning to publish a monograph on the subject. In his papers on memory, by juxtaposing his clinical work with adult patients and his research on very young children, Kris sketched a trajectory of the dynamic relationship between preoedipal ego development and object relations, against the backdrop of psychosexual stages and the defenses that mediate memory. Kris was firmly committed to the premise that “the development of ego functions and object relations … are of equal and intrinsic importance.” This paper explores how Kris's papers on memory may also be read as delineating his thinking on the role of object relations in infants and young children. In his last paper, the posthumously published “Decline and Recovery in the Life of a Three-Year-Old” (1962), his interest in ego development and object relations shifts to object relations, but the role of memory remains crucial. The paper concludes with some observations about the omissions and distortions that characterize how the history of psychoanalysis is recalled, orally transmitted, and written.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Enlightenment Vision: An Overview

Morris Eagle

Morris Eagle

4351 Redwood Avenue, #1
Marina del Rey, CA 90292
E-mail: meagle100@aol.com

Morris Eagle   Professor Emeritus, Derner Institute for Advanced Psychological Studies, Adelphi University; Distinguished Educator in Residence, California Lutheran University; faculty, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; faculty, Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies.

Since this meeting marks the hundredth anniversary of the American Psychoanalytic Association, I thought it would be fitting to present an overview of some significant developments and trends in psychoanalysis during a good part of that period. My approach to the overview will be largely conceptual. That is, I will focus on broad conceptual trends rather than present a chronology of events.

A central trend I discuss is the increasing disjunction between psychoanalysis and the “Enlightenment vision” as expressed in a decreasing emphasis on the therapeutic value of interpretation, insight, and self-knowledge. A second and related trend I take up is the shift in emphasis from the intrapsychic to the interpsychic (Bolognini 2004), or what has been referred to as the move from a one-person to a two-person psychology. This shift is expressed in an increasing emphasis on the therapeutic relationship as the main ingredient in therapeutic action or, in Sullivan's words, on the “interpersonal field” (1953).

The third trend I discuss is increasing skepticism toward even the very possibility of discovering truths about the patient. The fourth topic is a reconceptualization of the function of interpretation, one that continues the link between psychoanalysis and the Enlightenment vision. And finally, I discuss the shift from psychoanalytic research concerned with broad areas of human functioning to a near exclusive focus on therapeutic outcome.

Plenary Address, American Psychoanalytic Association, San Francisco, June 9, 2011. Submitted for publication September 13, 2011.

In the last few years, the question of whether the techniques used in psychodynamic psychotherapies and psychoanalysis should be differentiated categorically or dimensionally has generated substantial discussion (Kächele 2010). In the field of psychoanalysis, a categorical differentiation between the technique of psychoanalysis and that of psychodynamic psychotherapy is still preferred, a differentiation that has not been scientifically demonstrated on the basis of empirical analysis of the techniques used in actual practice. The Psychotherapy Process Q-set method (PQS; Jones 2000) allows such a differentiation to be made.

The divergence between the ideal of a treatment orientation and its actual practice can be studied by the application of prototypes representing the ideal conception of a psychoanalytic hour. [[RunningHead]]

The Olympian Delusion

Ellen Pinsky

Ellen Pinsky

8 Maple Avenue
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: epinsky@comcast.net

Ellen Pinsky   Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Let us stop to ponder the magnitude of Freud's discovery. For centuries men and women have searched for mandrake roots and other substances from which a love potion could be brewed. And then a Jewish Viennese physician uncovered love's secret. There is indeed a way in which one human being can make another fall in love, and the prescription is remarkably simple.

—Martin Bergmann (1986, p. 30)

Nothing takes place between them except that they talk to each other.

—Sigmund Freud (1926, p. 187)

This is an essay on an unpleasant subject: a subject so painful that some within the discipline of psychoanalysis wince and turn away from it—the sexual exploitation of patients. The psychoanalytic situation is an audacious endeavor that purposely courts risk: for a time placing one human being as if at the center of another's emotional life. In that power-imbalanced relationship, behind closed doors, what is the patient's protection?

Lawrence Friedman (2008) says the following about Freud's Papers on Technique, six essays written between 1911 and 1915:

Papers on Technique floats the buoys that mark out psychoanalysis from other human relationships. You can argue about whether to steer this way or that around those markers, but without them you have nothing but open sea [p. 1032].

In these pages, I try to consider what the bouys mean, and the perils they aim to mark. In my view, Freud's most fundamental “buoy” is the principle of abstinence. I am concerned in this paper with abstinence and integrity, and the shoals and depths that endanger them. I hope to raise more questions than I answer.

The author is grateful for the suggestions and encouragement of many friends and colleagues in writing this essay, and thanks in particular, for their patient refinements and cogent suggestions and criticisms, Lisa Carbone, Nancy Chodorow, Lawrence Friedman, Louise Glück, Alexandra Harrison, Judy Kantrowitz, Catherine Kimble, James Longenbach, Janet Noonan, Robert Pinsky, Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau, Dawn Skorczewski, and Pam Wine. Submitted for publication October 13, 2010.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Institutions and Treatment Resistance: Commentary on Kafka

Edward Shapiro

Edward Shapiro

25 Main Street
Stockbridge, MA 01262
E-mail: edward.Shapiro@austenriggs.net

Edward Shapiro   Medical Director/CEO, Austen Riggs Center; Clinical Professor of Psychiatrry, Yale Child Study Center; Training and Supervising Analyst, Berkshire Psychoanalytic Institute.

John Kafka offers us a retrospective on an extraordinary institution committed to working with severely troubled patients who could not be treated elsewhere. The opportunity at Chestnut Lodge for psychoanalytically trained clinicians to see psychotic and characterologically disturbed patients intensively and over extended periods opened new understandings of the edges of human experience. The work of these clinicians demonstrated that the profound isolation inherent in psychosis could be overcome if someone was there to listen. Kafka underlines what it takes for clinicians to be “trained” by these patients to be listeners. Fromm-Reichman, Searles, Will, Pao, Kafka, and others from the Lodge unpacked patients' seemingly bizarre transferences and clinicians' often troubling countertransference feelings evoked in the context of prolonged and extensive psychotherapy. These discoveries opened the patients' efforts to communicate and illuminated their terror about being lost in the obliterating other through their intense longing. These ideas—and the related understanding of unconscious, nonverbal, interpersonally evoked communication—expanded the boundaries of psychoanalytic knowledge. Kafka's notion of “atmospheric objects” points to these patients' relatedness to their environment. The re-created childhood context that interfered with their psychological development was opened for examination and perspective.

Chestnut Lodge and other psychoanalytically focused hospitals were centrally important in expanding the understanding of severe disturbance. However, they left many issues unaddressed that offer opportunities for the next generation of psychoanalytic institutions. For example, Kafka does not speak to the impact of psychoactive medications in managing the acute phase of schizophrenia.

In Pursuit of Interpersonal Distance and Self-Reliance: Neural Substrates of Attachment System Deactivation in Avoidant Individuals

Sohye Kim , John Williams , Keith Edwards and Todd Hall

Todd Hall

1100 Bates Street, 4th Fir
Houston, TX 77030
E-mail: sohyekim@hotmail.com

Todd Hall  

In his classic trilogy, Bowlby (1969, 1973, 1980) claimed that the attachment system is an innate, universal behavioral system that is activated by physical and psychological threats and operates toward the goal of maintaining proximity to attachment figures. Recent studies (Mikulincer et al. 2000; Mikulincer, Gillath, and Shaver 2002) have used implicit cognitive tasks to document that symbolic threat stimuli heightened the accessibility of attachment-related cognition in the minds of adults. Anxiously attached adults exhibited heightened accessibility to attachment themes in both threat and non-threat contexts, reflecting their chronically activated attachment system. Avoidantly attached adults, in contrast, demonstrated increased access to attachment-related themes in attachment-unrelated threat contexts in comparison to non-threat contexts, while displaying decreased accessibility in attachment threat contexts.

Making a Difference in Patients' Lives: Emotional Experience in the Therapeutic Setting. By Sandra Buechler. Psychoanalysis in a New Key Book Series 8. New York: Routledge, 2008, xviii + 317 pp., $99.50 hardcover, $39.95 paperback.

Dwarakanath Rao

Dwarakanath Rao

555 East William Street, Suite 24-1
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: dgrao@umich.edu

Dwarakanath Rao  

In theory, every change in practice requires a change in theory. However, the analyst's personal proclivities, wishes for idiosyncratic satisfactions, and the many exigencies of everyday practice do not invite a steady integration of theory with practice. While we wait for innovations to find a place in theory, many are forced to use increasingly subjective indices, of immediacy, relevance, and efficiency, to guide our work. Some would say they are the calling cards of contemporary clinical work. Sandra Buechler, in Making a Difference in Patients' Lives: Emotional Experience in the Therapeutic Setting, describes how she works with emotions—her patients' and her own—to bring about change. In doing so, she attempts to lay the foundations of a general theory of emotions and to examine its relevance to the analyst's emotions in treatment.

Using interpersonal psychoanalysis, which is her stated training background, and a theory of emotions she ascribes mainly to Izard (1977),1 as well as the insights of a substantial clinical and scholarly career, she addresses several wide-ranging topics in this book. She begins with eight assumptions about emotions, many overlapping: emotions are separable into discrete and enumerated states, such as sadness and joy; each emotion alters every other emotion within an “emotion system”; cognition affects emotion, and vice versa; emotions are adaptive; each emotion has discrete motivational and species-specific importance (after Darwin); each person has an idiosyncratic history with each emotion due to personal and social experiences; and emotional life is frequently implicit, unformulated, and unverbalized.

Although none of the author's assumptions about emotions appear to be new, she sees them as providing a framework for clinical interventions she finds groundbreaking and curative. After describing the eight assumptions about emotions, Buechler leaves the dry shore of theory and wades into what seems like many oceans of experience all at once.

Boyhoods: Rethinking Masculinities. By Ken Corbett. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2009, 286 pp., $26.00.

Paula Bernstein

Paula Bernstein

4900 Cherry Creek South Drive, Suite D
Denver, CO 80246
E-mail: drpaulabernstein@gmail.com

Paula Bernstein  

Ken Corbett has been working at the cutting edge of feminism, queer studies, and gender theory for twenty years. In Boyhoods: Rethinking Masculinity, he brilliantly incorporates these perspectives into his clinical work with children and their parents. Corbett's plan is to reopen central premises regarding masculine development, hoping to lay the groundwork for a psychoanalytic theory that is closer to a boy's experience and reflective of the lives lived by boys (and men) in today's societies. The book transcends its mission, conveying the life and vitality of boyhood so lyrically that it will appeal to anyone aching to remember and to everyone who works with children.

Part I of the book, Boys, Masculinity, and the Family, reworks two papers first published in Psychoanalytic Quarterly. Each shows a boy's masculinity unfolding in relation to his particular set of parents and the society in which they live. In “Little Hans: Masculinity Foretold,” Corbett shows how Freud “parses and whittles” Hans's material, directing the father/analyst's interpretations to arrive at the normative oedipal narrative. Corbett's premise is that Freud “foretold” masculinity through this case, capturing certain aspects of a little boy's experience while excluding others—creating the “Ur-boy of psychoanalysis.” From Little Hans forward we know “a boy to be a boy through his phallic preoccupations and castration fears, enacted alongside and through his desire for his mother and his rivalry with his father, which in time resolve via the boy's separation from his mother and his identification with his father” (p. 19). Corbett finds an intriguing gap between Freud's emphasis on rivalry and aggression and the affective resonances of dependence and desire that emanate from the pages of the case history. “A theory of masculinity that is forged solely through competition with paternal authority, with little regard for the interplay of identifications, desire, and mutual recognition … is largely a theory of phallic narcissism-qua-masculinity” (p. 49).

Silvia Bell

Silvia Bell

1801 South Road
Baltimore, MD 21209
E-mail: smvbell@aol.com

Silvia Bell  

In their thoughtful introduction to this volume, the editors comment on “a marked tension between child psychotherapy and mainstream empirical research” that has persisted until recently (p. 4). Researchers have been dismissive of the potential for systematic study of the psychotherapeutic enterprise; clinicians have eschewed research, wary of invasive interventions that could distort the processes and outcomes under study. This view of research as inimical to clinical work has contributed to a lag in the development of research methods adequate to the task of studying the fluid processes characteristic of the psychotherapeutic endeavor. At a time when our profession is increasingly challenged to give proof of the effectiveness of our interventions, this collection of research papers is framed not just as a compilation of research findings but as evidence of the intrinsic benefits of research to the development of our theory and the systematizing of our practice. Cognizant that the methods of traditional experimental science are inadequate, the editors aim to present a “selection of pioneering studies” that exemplifies the richness of methodologies now available to investigate psychotherapeutic intervention in a manner both scientifically rigorous and clinically valid (p. 11). The book is organized into four parts: Part I expands our consideration of what constitutes research in the clinical setting by contrasting two methodological approaches; Part II includes four examples of psychotherapy process research; Part III focuses on studies of outcome changes and clinical effectiveness of child psychotherapy; Part IV presents some contributions of child psychotherapy research in the wider context of related disciplines. The editors state that they aim “to highlight the considerations that must be addressed if research is to do justice to the distinctiveness of the psychoanalytic approach” (p. 10).

Anna Aragno

140 West End Avenue, 23C
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: Annaragno@earthlink.net

Anna Aragno  

Even as we contemplate the possibility that the Hubble Distant Field has identified the earliest star-forming galaxies, enabling us to peer back to the beginnings of time, expert astronomers tell us, that's not far enough! Similarly, if you were thinking that perhaps as a species we have tapped the outer limits of disciplines designed to increase our knowledge and ability to manipulate nature, think again—up pops a whole new area of

1288

research and scholarship: a point of view? a discipline? a gathering of academics posing similar questions? A cutting edge interdisciplinary field, about to become a science!

Biosemiotics: What is it? Where did it come from? and where does it hope to go? If you are curious about any of these questions, the book to turn to is Donald Favareau's lively, exhaustive anthology, Essential Readings in Biosemiotics. This is a definitive text (and source-guide) on the origins and development, the founders and facets, early and current writings, of the colorful cast of characters that formed and now convey this new multinational, interdisciplinary enterprise called biosemiotics. Along with Marcello Barbieri's collection Introduction to Biosemiotics (2008b), Favareau's lovingly compiled compendium provides the basic foundational knowledge that anyone interested in this new field needs to start out. And there is a lot to learn about the “bio” of semiosis, a way of looking at scientific metaphors of yore and uncovering their operative communicative processes. Biosemiotics belongs in an information paradigm oriented toward examining the “in between” of live interactions taking place all the time, at all systemic levels, within the general unity of life.

Given that the psychoanalytic method is based on semiotic and dialogic processes that not only index unconscious meanings but also uncover nonconscious forms of experience and thought, there may be good reason to dip into this biosemiotic discussion and consider its scientific/conceptual dimension. For Freud the deep unconscious was the biological substrate of primary process and psychic reality. Perhaps there is a natural bridge between the original Freudian agenda, set out in his papers on metapsychology, and the philosophical and epistemological avenues opened up by the approach of this new interdiscipline.

Repairing Links: Building Attachments in the Preschool Classroom

L. Keller

L. Keller

5435 College Avenue #201
Oakland, CA 94618
E-mail: dr.keller@kellerphd.com

L. Keller  

A psychoanalytic model of preschool consultation is presented, born of the conviction that psychoanalysis has powerful tools with which to tackle some of our most difficult and entrenched community problems. Since trauma is known to impact our ability to think reflectively and symbolically (Fonagy, Moran, and Target 1993), the clinical methods of psychoanalysis—drawing on the ideas of container/contained, the capacity to hold alternate points of view, and the capacity to reflect empathically (here with teachers in the face of their at times harsh and insensitive treatment of students)—are crucial to success in work with cumulatively traumatized staff (Khan 1963). The strongest resistances to consultation arise from an anti-attachment system developed as a result of cumulative trauma and operating at multiple levels in the preschool. Consultation services are organized around an understanding of this anti-attachment system. At various levels of the system, including staff, parents, and children, consultants work to facilitate secure relationships in the preschool setting. These relationships foster recognition of children's emotional complexities and build cooperative links between staff and parents, in an atmosphere that otherwise might often push adults into fearful, self-protective states that interfere with their contact with children's states of mind.

To put one brick upon another,

Add a third, and then a fourth,

What you do has any worth.

Psychoanalysis and the Psychoses: Commentary on Kafka

Elyn Saks and Orrin Evans

Orrin Evans

699 Exposition Boulevard
Los Angeles, CA 90089
E-mail: esaks@law.usc.edu

Orrin Evans   Professor of Law, Psychology, and Psychiatry and the Behavioral Sciences, University of Southern California Gould School of Law; Adjunct Professor of Psychiatry, UC-San Diego School of Medicine; faculty, New Center for Psychoanalysis.

John Kafka's article is an extremely interesting musing upon the psychoanalytic treatment of patients with psychosis in the context of a psychoanalytic hospitalization, namely, at Chestnut Lodge. A hotbed of therapeutic and research activity, the Lodge provided therapists both a nurturing and a stimulating group process around treating patients with psychosis, a process that helped not only the individual patients but also the analysts and the field itself. Many interesting ideas arose in the context of work in this institution. Some of the prime questions raised remain with us today.

Rather than address Kafka's article point by point, I intend to discuss my own take on some of the issues he raises. I note also that I speak not as a researcher into the psychoanalytic treatment of people with psychosis, nor as a reader and purveyor of theories in articles by others on psychoanalysis of the psychoses. Rather, I speak as a patient with psychosis who has benefited enormously from psychoanalytic treatment, four or five times a week for over three decades (Saks 2007). I do this recognizing that I am an n of only one, so that my experience may not generalize to that of others; and that I may be wrong when I speculate even about my own case.

The Continuum Question

The first issue I'd like to address is whether there is a continuum between neurosis and psychosis. Kafka suggests this would be the case if the conflict theory of symptoms applies to both. But many think that conflict cannot explain psychosis—we must rather rely on the concept of deficit.

Kay Long

Kay Long

234 Church Street, Suite 300
New Haven, CT 06510-1804
E-mail: kay.long@yale.edu

Kay Long  

Michael Feldman, a leading figure in the London group of Kleinian analysts, has developed a powerful and remarkably coherent approach to clinical theory and technique in his work of the past twenty-five years. In Doubt, Conviction and the Analytic Process, Feldman pays unparalleled attention to the impact of the patient's communications on the mind of the analyst at work. These thirteen interrelated papers—some published in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, others presented here for the first time—address a central question: to what extent does any experience in analysis—doubt, conviction, splitting, projective identification, reassurance, compliance, grievance—“reflect processes within the analyst, to what extent do they derive from the patient's pressure and projections, how do these elements interact, and what are their possible effects on the movement in the session?” (p. 232). Written with exceptional clarity and immediacy, this book enables us to follow the evolution of Feldman's thought as he expands existing theory to contend with the clinical dilemmas encountered by all analysts. As Roy Schafer writes in his introduction, “One has the sense of being welcomed into the workshop of a master analyst to reflect with him as he works on key concepts” (p. xiii).

Feldman's unique perspective is to view whatever topic he is writing about through the lens of the moment-to-moment interactions between analyst and patient in the analytic session. He has the ability to “see a world in a grain of sand” while never losing sight of the patient's history and character, the hour as a whole, or the phase of the analysis. Emphasizing the centrality of unconscious fantasies, Feldman maps a route to psychic change through technical approaches deeply grounded in the theories of Freud, Klein, and his contemporary Kleinian colleagues. He also draws from unexpected sources, such as Joseph Sandler and Ernst Kris. Feldman often begins at a point of impasse in the analytic encounter and, to produce movement, develops theory and technique in light of what he has discovered in his fine-grained view. While Feldman focuses on analytic process and technique throughout this volume, his substantial theoretical advances on topics such as projective identification, envy, and the death drive are equally impressive.

Light and Shadow in the Life and Work of Louise Nevelson

Laurie Wilson

Laurie Wilson

175 West 12th Street 6A
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: laurie.wilson@nyu.edu

Laurie Wilson   Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, The Institute for Psychoanalytic Education Affiliated with NYU School of Medicine; Professor Emerita, NYU graduate art therapy program.

The life and work of the artist Louise Nevelson are examined through the lens of her ideas about light and shadow. Nevelson developed several ways of dealing with a basic fault established in childhood. Some ideas of Christopher Bollas are used in understanding the artist's relationships with art dealers who served her as transformational objects. The artist became the grandmother of environmental sculpture in America and saw her work as a bridge between the third and the fourth dimension—an idealized realm and a popular concept in twentieth-century art that she related directly to light and shadow. Colette Roberts, a dealer with whom she worked, allowed Nevelson to arrive at a signature style. Arne Glimcher, who represented her for twenty-four years, provided the material and psychological comfort that allowed Nevelson to explore new materials and new styles and to arrive at aesthetic solutions of remarkable power and sophistication through her eighty-ninth year. From the time she met Roberts in 1953 until the end of her life thirty-five years later, Nevelson's ideas about light and shadow expressed not only her thoughts about visual perception but her experience of her mother as her original transformational object and unthought known.

The shadow is as important as the object. … I arrest it and I give it architecture as solid as anything can be.

—Louise Nevelson (in Lipman 1983)

Anna Badini  

Lucio Russo, a prominent Italian psychoanalyst, has undertaken a timely study of human identity through what he calls an exploratory journey of the human condition: life and death, illusion and reality, love and hate. He has investigated the formation and the vicissitudes of identity by applying the psychoanalytic theoretical instruments and method to literary experiences that are viewed as exemplary of the current era, and by creating an exciting dialogue among psychoanalytic theoreticians, literary authors, and philosophers.

The product of Russo's exploration is Destini delle identitè (Destinies of the Identities), a 240-page book published in Italian by Borla in 2009. This book is written with penetrating precision, mastery, and love of psychoanalysis and literature, and is dense with rich and profound insights and captivating illustrations. Informed by an undeniably Freudian drive theory perspective, it brings together well-known classical theories with recent insights, such as those of Green and de M'Uzan. But it remains embedded in exclusively European and British theoretical soil.
