The present psychoanalytic era seems to feature an unprecedented level of anxiety among analysts concerning the future. In particular, psychoanalytic practice seems to be in jeopardy. Practice numbers continue their downward tilt in many locales. A national leader suggests that the American Psychoanalytic Association is “in decline.” To paraphrase Marvell, at his back the psychoanalyst always hears the “evidenced-based” chariot hurrying near. Psychoanalysts face “marginalization,” or have already been marginalized.

Paul Stepansky's Psychoanalysis at the Margins is an exploration of the causes of this marginalization, and a consideration of the prospects of American psychoanalysis, both its theory and its practice. Stepansky offers the personal experiences of a psychoanalytic publisher, as well as a full-scale synthesis and critique of the loss of authority of psychoanalysis within American psychiatry and as a treatment in the public eye. Taken together, Stepansky's background as historian of ideas, his intimate knowledge of the publishing process, and his own considerable published scholarship not only on Freud, Adler, and others, but also his remarkable grasp of the history of modern medicine—all give his perspective on the field a unique authority in considering the trajectory of psychoanalytic practice in the U.S.

Psychoanalysis at the Margins opens as a memoir of Stepansky's efforts to keep the Analytic Press going. His reader hears firsthand an account of a venture that began with great promise and ended with frustration as press runs for new books progressively dwindled. Stepansky contextualizes the history of this press within the much longer history of psychoanalytic publishing and its influence in the U.S., in the process providing remarkable documentation of total book sales, which suggest the extent of the influence of psychoanalytic ideas among the general public. This influence began in the 1930s but reached its height in the sixties and seventies. Memorable items of information Stepansky documents include the likelihood that the three most published psychoanalytic authors of all time in the U.S. are Charles Brenner, Erich Fromm, and Erik Erikson, who collectively sold over ten million books. Fromm's titles in print likely outnumber those of Brenner's and Erikson's combined, by a ratio of five to one, but Brenner and Erikson each probably sold at least a million copies of their works.1

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl  

Philosophia, as the ancient Greeks imagined it, was an exploration of all things, the whole cosmos, by a person who loved wisdom and wished to live a life in which that philia was uninhibited by any lack of internal psychophysical balance (harmonia) or any external oppression. Philosophia always included a therapeutics to resolve any internal imbalance—“moderation in all things” was a common prescription—and to overcome external obstacles, particularly obstacles to political freedom. For example, among the earliest Greek philosophers who left written texts were the followers of a doctor, Hippocrates of Cos, whose therapeutics included dream interpretation to diagnose imbalances, exercise, a dietary regime to adjust the body's four humors, and dramas (tragic and comic) performed at their spas to purge extreme disbalancing emotions and teach lessons in preventing tyranny in family life and in the political realm. Hippocrates' moral wisdom was very succinct; it consisted of the imperative “Do no harm.”

Aristotle seems to have been the first philosopher to make an orderly catalogue of all the areas of exploration the philosopher should take up, viewing each area as a microcosm of the macrocosmic “all things,” and viewing each area as an instance of the principle he thought universal: that all things evolve or mature from potentiality to actuality, from simpler to more complex (but also more orderly) forms. At his school, the Lyceum, Aristotle lectured on all the types of study he had catalogued, from metaphysics through practical philosophy—ethics, politics, rhetoric—and on to particular sciences. He ranged over all the areas we associate with the natural sciences (displaying intense love for biology and zoology), the social sciences, and the humanities. The Greek philosophers thought of their own minds-and-bodies and all they studied around them as microcosmoi through which they could explore the macrocosmos

Adele Tutter

Adele Tutter

Adele Tutter   Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University School of Physicians and Surgeons; Clinical Adjunct Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Philip Johnson's masterpiece—the Glass House—is compared to a dream and conceptualized as containing encrypted and embedded representations of the self. Freud's masterpiece—The Interpretation of Dreams—is the theoretical and methodological model for this approach to design-as-dream. Drawing on Johnson's words and forms set in biographical, historical, and cultural context, interpretive paths are traced from manifest design elements of the Glass House to overdetermined latent meanings, yielding new and surprising insights into the Glass House, its elusive architect, and the process of its design. A mirror that reflects an image, a lens that focuses it, and a prism that reveals its components, the Glass House turns a lucid eye onto its maker.

Ruth Fischer  

The sixty-third edition of Psychoanalytic Study of the Clild, dedicated to the memory of Peter Neubauer, continues in its tradition of theoretical and clinical excellence, as well as of extending the purview of psychoanalysis. Both classical and cutting-edge concepts and understandings are explored.

214

This volume is an antidote to the widespread sense of despair so many of us experience in contemplating the future of child analysis as we note the scarcity of child analytic cases and the limited opportunity we have to engage in it. Here we see child analysis alive and well, and at its best. We see how important it is for it to thrive, to buck the trend toward treatments aimed at specific symptoms, with no appreciation of the role of development and the complexity and importance of the whole individual.

Although this is evident in all of the contributions, it is most clearly underlined in an article by Luyten and colleagues, “Equifinality, Multifinality and the Rediscovery of the Importance of Early Experiences: Pathways from Early Adversity to Psychiatric and (Functional) Somatic Disorders.” The authors decry the disorder-centered approach, noting that pathways to the end state are complex, the result of multiple developmental pathways (multifinality) or similar factors leading to dissimilar outcomes (equifinality). This idea, which underlies current research in psychoanalysis, neuroscience, and genetics, counters popular reductionism. Individuals experiencing early trauma are highly vulnerable to a wide variety of somatic and psychological disorders.

If we can abandon the misplaced effort to locate the specific etiological agent of each specific disorder, we are free to consider complex interactions between early and later life experiences, inner and outer conflicts, and their interactions with biological endowment. It frees us to consider the therapeutic encounter and wonder about the transformation that allows for an opening up of development. This topic is pursued in the volume's Research Section, in which an in-depth effort is made by a study group to delineate and define transformations that occur in the analytic process. This psychoanalytic perspective leads to a greater understanding of vulnerability and resilience. Nonspecific factors such as the patient-therapist alliance may be more important than specific factors such as a drug or treatment protocol. This is a shift toward a more person-oriented research strategy.

It is a difficult task to review a book that presents the results of nearly forty years of dedication to a program of investigation, one solidly grounded on methodological decisions set well in advance as part of a way of modeling research. A method is a road that one can judge either from the inside, by traveling it, or from the outside. Because most of the text has been published previously, as the work progressed, many readers will already have formed an impression of the results and modes of research undertaken by the contributors (centered around Helmut Thomä and Horst Kächele).

A global look at the theoretical and clinical material gathered in these pages yields an impressive view—a lifelong endeavor documented with great zeal and offered to the consideration of colleagues for scrutiny and discussion. To do justice to such achievement, to show due respect to the intellectual effort and the consistency of the attempt, it would be necessary at the very least to produce a text of equal size and thoroughness. Totaling 470 pages, this work contains seven sections: Psychoanalytic Therapy Process Research (20 pages), Problems of Metascience and Methodology in Clinical Psychoanalytic Research (78 pages), The Significance of the Case History in Clinical Psychoanalytic Research (44 pages), Amalia X, the German Psychoanalytic Specimen Case (78 pages), Guided Clinical Judgments (112 pages), Linguistic Studies (58 pages), and A Summary and Implications of Research for Psychoanalytic Practice (14 pages).

Each section is scrupulously presented with an abundance of references (54 pages in all, around 1000 entries, at the back of the book). But as massive as the achievement may seem, the book does not attempt to be encyclopedic; instead it is written from a chosen perspective, selective and personal, a singular way of looking at things. That is to say that many contributions relevant to the subject are left aside, and that differing points of view are considered critically, with the aim of backing up the authors' own way of conceptualizing and practicing research and psychoanalysis.

Therapist Variables and Patient Outcome after Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Caspar Berghout and Jolien Zevalkink

Jolien Zevalkink

Olympiaplein 4
1076 AB Amsterdam
The Netherlands
E-mail: berghout@npsai.nl

Jolien Zevalkink  

Evidence from studies that have focused on the effects of therapist variables on treatment outcome suggests that a moderate amount of variance in patient outcomes is attributable to therapist differences, regardless of the type of

577

treatment practiced. In the literature, researchers have considered gender of the therapist, therapists' experience and training, therapists' treatment attitudes, and self-reported attachment style to be relevant therapist variables (e.g., Beutler et al. 2004). In the present study, we investigated whether these therapist variables are related to patient outcome in a group of patients after psychoanalysis and a group of patients after psychoanalytic psychotherapy.

Method

The patient sample (N = 97) originated from a project, with participants from four mental health care organizations in the Netherlands, designed to study the effectiveness of long-term psychoanalytic treatment. For the present study, we focused on patients who had ended long-term psychoanalytic treatment. One group of patients had received psychoanalysis (PA; n = 40); the other group had received psychoanalytic psychotherapy (PP; n = 57). Patient outcome was assessed by using the three outcome factors that were found in the PCA factor analyses: General distress, Introversion, and Disadaptation and disorganization (see Berghout, Zevalkink, and de Jong 2010).

All therapists (N=53) in the project were licensed clinicians (psychiatrist-psychotherapists or psychologist-psychotherapists) and members of one of the Netherlands psychoanalytic societies. Of the 53 therapists, 33 were female (62%) and 20 were male (38%). The mean age was 54.5 years (SD = 7.5). The average length of therapeutic experience was 24.3 years (SD = 10.7), where the number of years after licensing (M = 17.2, SD = 9.3) was greater than the number of years before licensing (M = 7.1, SD = 6.3).

The Therapeutic Attitudes Scales-2 (TASC-2; Sandell et al. 2004) was used to assess therapist variables.

Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI 48009
E-mail: nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish  

This book is an eye- and mind-popping study of self-representational art by ten female artists, whose groundbreaking works span the last century. Thus, it documents the changes in art, in the surrounding culture, and in the experience of women in that time frame. Its author, Danielle Knafo, a clinical psychologist, psychoanalyst, and art critic, has undertaken a monumental feat of scholarship, which she says took fifteen years to complete. Knafo has chosen women she identifies with and admires in terms of their personal courage, strength, and revolutionary spirit: “Each artist, it now seems, has told me something central about myself, about being a woman and facing the things a woman must face, not only by virtue of her gender but also because she is a human being living in a cultural time and place. So, I see their lives and work as tracing a scintillating trajectory not only through female self-representational art but also through the last century of our history” (p. 171).

The ten artists are the German Kathe Kollwitz (1867-1945), who departed from established, idealized depictions of the female body to painting women's faces tortured by war, poverty, and loss; the French-born Claude Cahun (1894-1954), a little-known surrealist photographer, artist, and political activist whose work challenged and expanded the boundaries of gender; Mexico's famous Frida Kahlo (1907-1954), whose bold paintings represented women's unique experiences and pain; Charlotte Salomon (1917-1943), German-born, killed in Auschwitz, who transformed the terrible traumas of her young life into a moving and amazingly multimedia work; the American Carolee Schneemann (b. 1939), who launched female performance art when minimalist art was at its peak and asserted the validity of female sexual pleasure; another American, Hannah Wilke (1940-1993)

The Vienna Jazz Trio: A Novel. By Tomas Böhm. Charlottesville, VA: Pitchstone Publishing, 2010, 272 pp., $17.95.

Tomas Böhm and Braxton McKee

Braxton McKee

255 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: bmckee@comcast.net

Braxton McKee  

Tomas Böhm's The Vienna Jazz Trio begins with the central character's consultation with Freud in 1920. What follows is a gripping and moving tale that takes us through the rise of Hitler, the Holocaust, and on into the postwar period in this country.

To do this, Böhm, a Swedish psychoanalyst, focuses on the formation and evolution of the Vienna Jazz Trio. We, the readers, are engaged by these men, their trio, and their unfolding lives. We see Vienna through their eyes and come to a new appreciation of what it was like to be there during the time Hitler rose to power. We come to know their friends and families and to get a sense of their experience too. As the nightmare unfolds we share their protest, and their desperation. In the story, by chance, the trio find themselves in England just as their families are trapped in Vienna. We see them suffer the anguish and guilt of those who managed to escape and were, as a consequence, cut off from their families. We see, too, the crushing impact on them as they learn of the fates of those they love.

Central to the telling of this story is the story of jazz—which the author clearly loves and knows well. Jazz is a major character in this novel. It provides a way of talking about the affinity between the oppressed Jews of Europe and the oppressed Blacks of this country. It becomes a way of talking of the way in which improvisation, central to jazz, becomes a central tool in their survival, and in their ultimate revenge.

Freud, too, figures in the story. It is through Freud, nicely realized by Böhm, that Nathan, our central character, meets Ernest Jones. And it is through Jones that the trio, like Freud, are able to get to the safety of England.

Böhm has found an ingenious way of revisiting a dreadful time in recent history. The story he tells vividly reminds us of those times and their terror. At the same time, it is his story of jazz that, in a way, gives us hope.

Richard Honig

3001 Highland Avenue

Richard Honig  

For most readers this posthumously published collection of fourteen essays will likely be a first introduction to the work of Jean-Georges Schimek, an extraordinary psychoanalytic scholar and theoretician. Schimek's talent is to trace the evolution of such fundamental Freudian constructs as psychic reality, unconscious fantasy, unconscious representation, and transference and to discern the many ambiguities, complexities, and contradictions that are the inevitable accompaniment of Freud's fecundity. His critique of Freud is presented in such a manner that the theoretical and clinical dilemmas he explores can be brought into discourse with the same issues extant in current psychoanalytic theoretical debate.

The essays collected here originally appeared between 1964 and 2000. Five were published between 1973 and 1987, and several others were presentations to the Rapaport-Klein Study Group, of which he was a member. The final chapter, a superb exegesis of The Interpretation of Dreams, was not published in his lifetime, due to his concern that the questions he raised about Freud's conception of unconscious fantasy might be used by Freud bashers claiming that Freud had distorted the data of childhood seduction to fit his theory.

Entering this text, we hear the influence of Schimek's mentors and contemporaries, especially Erikson, whom he first encountered at Berkeley and subsequently followed to Austen Riggs, where he was a psychology fellow from 1956 to 1959.

Luis Kancyper is a training psychoanalyst at the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association and a prolific author. He has worked for many years on subjects related to theories of narcissism and the death instinct. His work not only manifests a thorough and profound understanding of Freud, but makes original contributions of its own.

His recent book, Resentimiento terminable e interminable: Psicoanálisis y literatura, was published in 2010. Though rooted primarily in Freud, Kancyper integrates this thinking with concepts from Melanie Klein, Jacques Lacan, André Green, D. W. Winnicott, Willie Baranger, and Hanna Arendt, as well as with his own ideas. What emerges is a highly original book loaded with enlightening theoretical concepts.

Kancyper believes that literature's profound descriptions of the torments and conflicts afflicting the human soul contribute significantly to psychoanalytic understanding. Here he seeks to wrest theoretical understanding from literature by combining psychoanalytic theory and clinical material with the works of Albert Camus, Jorge Luis Borges, Franz Kafka, and Sándor Márai. This makes for reading that it is both intellectually stimulating and emotionally enjoyable.

His focus is on the affect of rancor. He writes about the power of resentment and remorse from both a theoretical and a clinical perspective. Kancyper explains that people have different ways of dealing with frustrations, leading them to be “inhabited” by various types of memories. In addition to rancor, he writes about memories of terror and of pain.

When people are inhabited by the memory of rancor, they feel resentment and a sense of having been victims of unfairness. Such people feel special and privileged in their victimhood. They are arrogant and want revenge. They feel compelled to settle the score for a past they cannot forget. Incapable of mourning, they live the present in terms of an unsettled past.

The Neurobiology of Fear Memory Reconsolidation and Psychoanalytic Theory

Jack Gorman and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

2700 Arlington Avenue
Bronx, NY 10463
E-mail: jack.gorman@alumni.upenn.edu

Steven Roose   Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, Department of Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University; Research Chair, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Research Psychiatrist, New York State Psychiatric Institute.

Advances in both experimental neuroscience and psychoanalytic theory and technique have made it possible to consider mechanisms by which psychodynamic psychotherapies might have an impact at the cellular and molecular level. Here potential analogies are drawn between (1) the mechanisms and results of blocking the reconsolidation of conditioned fear memories in the laboratory and (2) several key aspects of psychoanalytic process. A review of the biology of conditioned fear memory, including differences between extinction and inhibition of reconsolidation, indicates that this biology may have relevance to various ways in which psychoanalytic therapy is effective. The ideas proposed here might lead to further experimental attempts to understand the molecular biology of psychoanalysis.

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford

Bret Rutherford  

First elaborated by Freud in the late 1890s, the concept of unconscious fantasy has been a central element of psychoanalytic theories of pathogenesis and therapeutic action ever since. These unconscious wishes, fears, memories of early experiences, and scripts for interaction with significant others are postulated to organize the data of perception and to influence an individual's conscious experience and behavior. Until recently, clinical data have been the sole means of obtaining information about unconscious fantasies: dreams, symptoms, slips of the tongue, and repetitive or hoped-for object relations. Similarly, psychoanalytic or psychotherapeutic treatment has been the primary means of uncovering pathological unconscious fantasies and remedying their maladaptive effects on a person's life. As has occurred with other psychoanalytic constructs described in Journal Watch, neuroscience has begun to help us understand the biological underpinnings of unconscious fantasy.

It has long been known that depressed individuals exhibit a “mood-congruent negative processing bias,” which is to say they preferentially attend to, predict, and recall negatively valenced stimuli—the glass is always half empty. The amygdala has been hypothesized to play a key role in mediating this processing bias, as experiments have shown that depressed patients have amygdala hyperactivation (relative to controls)

Catherine Sullivan  

In graduate school, a classmate of mine wrote her thesis about body modification. As I listened to her present her research to a standing-room-only crowd, I remember wondering why so many people were interested in this seemingly remote subject; how would this material be relevant as I ventured out into clinical practice? Almost fifteen years later, I approached Alessandra Lemma's Under the Skin: A Psychoanalytic Study of Body Modification with some of the same skepticism. Now, having finished the book, I am a skeptic no longer.

Body modification is the deliberate altering of the body for nonmedical reasons. This groundbreaking book is an in-depth psychoanalytic exploration of what motivates a person to modify his or her body. Lemma contends that modifications of the body's surface are born out of deep unconscious meaning. Lemma looks “under the skin” to the individual's internal life and to relationships with internalized objects to make sense of compulsive and extreme forms of body modification.

There is nothing new about our current-day body modification practices. Tattooing has been around since the Stone Age, and the first recorded plastic surgery technique was performed in India in 1000 B.C. to reattach an amputated nose (p. 132). Cross-cultural studies show a universal tendency to alter, distort, and disfigure the human body (p. 15). Despite these long-standing and universal tendencies, there has been a worldwide increase in body modification practices over the last twenty years. Cosmetic surgery has increased by 465 percent since 1997 (p. 134). In In the Flesh: The Cultural Politics of Body Modification (2003), Victoria Pitts details how in the late 1980s body piercing and tattooing took off as a countercultural movement. Subculture groups with diverse interests began to identify themselves as “marked persons” or body modifiers: “What they shared is that they all positioned the body as a site of exploration as well as a space needing to be reclaimed from culture” (p. 7). Since then, however, body piercing and tattooing have entered the mainstream. The worldwide increase in body modification, in tandem with the relative lack of focus in the psychoanalytic literature on the body self, makes Under the Skin a timely addition to our psychoanalytic discourse.

The history of psychoanalysis has a complicated and ambivalent relationship with addictions and their treatment, particularly when the addict has been a psychoanalyst. Early studies on the psychoanalytic treatment of addiction include work by Abraham (1908), Fenichel (1945), and Radó (1933). Hopkins (2001) described Masud Khan's alcoholism, and Dadoun (1995) wrote about the cocaine addiction of Otto Gross. Byck (1974) edited and introduced Freud's Cocaine Papers. Later contributions to the literature on the psychoanalytic treatment of addiction (Director 2005; Dodes 2002, 2010; Khantzian 1995; Kohut 1977; Krystal 1977; Levin 1991; Loose 2002; Wurmser 1978)

The Psychoanalytic Approach to Psychosis: Commentary on Kafka

Richard Munich

Richard Munich

286 Madison Avenue
PH 23
New York, NY 10017
E-mail: armunich@msn.com

Richard Munich   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Perhaps the most salient fact to describe the dramatic reduction in dynamically oriented, therapeutic community-based hospital treatment for patients with psychotic disorders is that there are fewer than a handful of facilities that still provide it as their mainstay. Even if one includes patients with concurrent Axis I and Axis II diagnoses, a significant percentage of that original hospital population, today there are far fewer admissions with a length of stay longer than six weeks. Pharmacological advances, so-called evidence-based treatments, economic factors, and longer-term outcome studies have provided various rationales for this reduction.

John Kafka has elegantly reviewed the contributions of the pre-reduction era at Chestnut Lodge, contributions echoed by those from similar institutions including Sheppard and Enoch Pratt Hospial, McLean Hospital, the Yale Psychiatric Institute, the Menninger Clinic, the Westchester Division of New York Hospital, the Austen Riggs Center, and the Institute of Living, among others. It is notable that he cites no papers on the issue of extended inpatient treatment for psychotic disorders after the early 1990s. With the exception of the International Society for the Psychological Treatments of the Schizophrenias and Other Psychoses, there is no organized academic or research support for the dynamically oriented psychotherapeutic treatment of these disorders. Current treatment of the most profound psychotic disorders is in the main organized around psychopharmacology, cognitive-behavioral therapies, psychiatric rehabilitation, psychoeducation, and assertive community treatment. Efforts to provide and shape inpatient treatment options for seriously disturbed patients in the current environment have been addressed elsewhere (Munich 2003; Munich and Allen 2003).

The author acknowledges the help of Elizabeth Bowman and Rebecca Chaplan with this commentary.

Common Ground Between Psychoanalysis and Art: Introduction to Wilson and Miller

Janice Lieberman

Janice Lieberman

E-mail: janicelieberman@gmail.com

Janice Lieberman  

Psychoanalysis is a listening profession. We psychoanalysts make meaning of our patients' words by using our ears and aural pathways to the brain. A number of years ago, I wrote about the use of alternative pathways for understanding our patients: “Responses to visual representations can be at times more visceral and immediate than they are to auditory communications. Visual sensations tend to be experienced more directly, and thus can short-circuit the kinds of intellectualizations and denial to which words lend themselves” (Lieberman 2000, p. 223).

We can train our eyes to better understand others (and ourselves as well) by looking at art and in that process give our ears, and the part of the brain to which they transmit, a temporary rest. Experiencing and understanding the content of art, how it was created, what went on in the minds and developmental histories of the artists, and tuning into what the artworks evoke in us (an analogue of the countertransference) cannot help but inform our clinical work, as well as enriching us personally. As Miller notes in the paper that follows, “familiar analytic ideas seem revitalized when they reveal themselves in art, [and] our understanding of them deepens” (p. 111).

Freud's seminal writings on Leonardo and Michelangelo have inspired analysts too numerous to list here to apply their psychoanalytic knowledge to art. Ellen Handler Spitz (1982) has identified three typical approaches to the psychoanalytic understanding of art:

pathography: understanding the neurosis of the artist as reflected in the artwork (an approach exemplified by Freud)—Freudian

interpreting the artwork as text itself and as representative of the relatively autonomous functioning of the ego (exemplified by Kris)—ego psychological

understanding the effect of the artwork on the viewer (an aesthetic approach exemplified by Winnicott)—Kleinian

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: ahesman@earthlink.net

Aaron Esman  

Psychoanalysts have been struggling with the mystery of art and the artist ever since Freud ventured into the field with “Leonardo” (1910) and laid down his formulation of the artist's flight from “reality” (1911). Each innovation in psychoanalytic theory has been accompanied by a new set of ideas about the creative process and the psychology of the artist—Otto Rank, Ernst Kris, and Heinz Kohut, along with some of their followers, being among the many who have ventured onto this rocky terrain. They are, of course, not alone; philosophers, cognitive psychologists, and, most recently, evolutionists (e.g., Dutton 2009) have all tried their hands at explaining how and why artists do what they do and how and why the rest of respond as we do to their efforts.

The most recent turn in our field is, of course, that of relational psychoanalysis, which appears to have assimilated earlier concepts of interpersonal psychology, British object relations theory, Kohutian self psychology, and attachment theory into an all-embracing system that tends to minimize the role of “classical” ideas about drives and intrapsychic conflict and emphasizes the critical influence of the vicissitudes of early child-parent relationships in the formation of potentially conflictual self systems.

Such, in any case, is the position of George Hagman, who in this challenging book sets forth what he proposes as “a new psychoanalytic aesthetic model that has both clinical and historical significance” (back cover). Through the study of the lives and works of a selected group of modern avant-garde artists— Edgar Degas, Henri Matisse, Joseph Cornell, Frank Lloyd Wright, Marcel Duchamp, Jackson Pollock, and Andy Warhol—he ventures to account for both their characters and their unique artistic achievements, as well as their particular roles in the history and evolution of modern art, in purely relational terms.

Hagman's method is well exemplified in his chapter on Degas. In its break with earlier tradition, “modern art,” he says, “has as its primary goal the enhancement, cure and restoration of the self (p. 38). (From another perspective one could, no doubt, say that its primary goal is the creation of beautiful or arresting objects, whatever the dictates of the individual artist's “self-psychology.”)

Richard Fox

Richard Fox

226 Monarch Bay Drive
Dana Point, CA 92629
E-mail: rpfoxmd@aol.com

Richard Fox  

Initially psychoanalytic theory and technique informed and helped shape that of psychotherapy. In Freud's words, analysis was the “pure gold” that was blended with suggestion to create the “bronze” (later reduced to baser metals) of psychotherapy.

With the progression of analytic thought from a one-person to a two-person psychology and the innovations of object relations theory, self psychology, and the intersubjective and relational points of view, the connection between the two practices has become a two-way street in which each stands to benefit from the other.

These four books, standing at the interface between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy, demonstrate to analysts and therapists alike what they stand to gain from careful consideration of the data provided by thoughtful study of psychodynamic or psychoanalytic psychotherapy. For the analyst they provide a fresh perspective on the interaction and therapeutic process in the analytic situation.

The books reviewed here provide an impressive demonstration of the vitality and significance of recent work in the fields of psychodynamic psychotherapy. The first two titles offer introductions to clinical psychotherapy for beginning and seasoned practitioners alike.

Emmett Wilson  

For a long time I've wondered how one might reconcile two seemingly disparate approaches to the unconscious: that of Freud and that of Proust. Now comes an interesting approach that might possibly move toward such a reconciliation, but one that may well involve some serious rethinking and reorientation. One of the pleasures of intellectual growth is divesting oneself of various prejudices of one's youth, such as that Puccini's music is just gorgeous schmaltz, Horowitz and Rubinstein were merely mindless technicians, Tchaikovsky's music just lush romanticism. It may also be that we will have to come to terms with some thinkers regarded, at least by the more positivistic among us, as “undisciplined mystics”: Hegel, Husserl, Heidegger, Gadamer, Jaspers, even Merleau-Ponty. These thinkers may yet have much to say to us.

There has been a mythological and distorted notion of psychoanalysis (to which psychoanalysis has itself often contributed) in which it is regarded as a discourse capable of expressing—without residual—the truth. This ingenuous, deterministic vision of psychoanalysis has had its proponents. Some may once have thought of psychoanalysis as an exquisite instrument to delve into the past of a human being, an instrument that allowed one to recover an individual's past wie es eigentlich gewesen ist—as it actually was. But even Freud, the determinist, the positivist, the arriviste would-be scientist, who at times seemed to think he had truth by the tail, gave up at certain points, for he not only acknowledged the biological bedrock, the uncanny, the inscrutable, the limits of analyzability, interminable analysis, and the tentative nature of his theories, but was even tempted by parapsychology and phylogenetic explanations.

Psychoanalytically Informed Psychotherapy of Psychosis: The Influences of American Psychoanalysis

David Garfield

David Garfield

600 Burton Avenue
Highland Park, IL 60035
E-mail: david.garfield@rosalindfranklin.edu

David Garfield  

Although Freud (1900, 1914, 1924) was fascinated by the psychoses, their relation to dreaming and to the profound alterations within the topographic and, later, the structural model, he thought their treatment was impossible because these were “narcissistic neuroses.” He was, no doubt, influenced by one of Bleuler's “four A's” of schizophrenia (1905), namely, “autism.” Finding in these conditions no libidinal cathexis of an external object, he concluded that the psychotic patient's inability to form a transference would thwart psychoanalytic work. Last spring in Washington, D.C., Eric Marcus assembled a diverse group of analysts who work psychoanalytically with psychotic patients to demonstrate that patients with psychosis can, contrary to this earlier view, make remarkable recoveries through psychoanalytic treatment. As we listen in on the panel, one can discern a decidedly “American” admixture regarding theory and technique when difficult challenges are encountered in the consulting room with this diverse and extraordinary group of patients.

Eric Marcus

Marcus opened the panel with a paper illustrating the use of the structural model to understand psychotic and “near psychotic” illness. His approach (Marcus 2003) focused on Hartmann's ideas of “the relatively autonomous ego functions” that help individuals distinguish between the emotional/cognitive, inside/outside, and fantasy/reality domains of psychic experience.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Washington, DC, June 13. Panelists: Eric Marcus (chair), Michael Garrett, Marlene Kocan, Andrew Lotterman.

Suttie's Influence on Fairbairn's Object Relations Theory

Graham Clarke

Graham Clarke

47 Lord Holland Road
Colchester, Essex CO2 7PS
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: graham@essex.ac.uk

Graham Clarke   Visiting Fellow, Centre for Psychoanalytic Studies, University of Essex.

It is suggested here that Ian Suttie influenced W. R. D. Fairbairn directly through a 1939 reprint of his The Origins of Love and Hate, a heavily underlined copy of which was found by the author in Fairbairn's library in the University of Edinburgh Library Special Collections in October 2009. Underlined sections of the book are compared with significant aspects of Fairbairn's post-1940 theorizing, and the similarities are argued to be due to Fairbairn's adopting many of the underlying attitudes and ideas that Suttie developed. This leaves Fairbairn's structural theory, which he began to develop as a rational reconstruction of Freud's structural theory as early as 1927, dependent in part on such theory but independent of direct influence by Suttie. It is argued that Suttie's importance vis-à-vis British object relations thinking needs to be reassessed.

Lamentation of an Aging Warrior: JAPA 2003-2011

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

As current editor of JAPA, I have had the role of guiding APsaA's scholarly journal through a period of challenge to all things psychoanalytic. JAPA has responded with format changes, new ventures, a broadening of theoretical allegiances on its editorial board, and other innovations designed to keep psychoanalytic scholarship current, responsive to adjacent disciplines, and welcoming of new talent and new perspectives. In this essay I will offer my take on what is new and important, what is old and still important, where JAPA has been and where it needs to go. A mix of celebration and manifesto, it is meant to convey the joys and challenges of psychoanalytic scholarship that make editing JAPA the fantastic experience it is.

On assuming the editorship in 2003, what quickly became apparent to me was that all my “good,” forward-looking ideas had origins in the work of the editors who preceded me at the helm of JAPA (see Blum 2011; Shapiro 2011; Richards 2011). Putting my stamp on the journal meant further developing ideas my predecessors had already introduced. That there are few truly new ideas is not only tempering of my ambitions, but is pertinent to considering the value of manuscripts not solely in relation to their novelty. Psychoanalysis is a conservative enterprise. Our knowledge is hard-won and requires constant defense against a public and a scientific community ever ready to dismiss what Freud referred to as his unsavory ideas. There are pieces that describe the already well known but do so in contemporary and compelling ways that deserve to be read and thought about. Fashions within psychoanalysis certainly change. The other day, a somewhat obsessional patient of mine was telling me about a carefully disguised struggle he was having with a colleague in his workplace. He characteristically spoke in a halting, overmethodical way, with frequent throat clearings, subliminal grunts and groans. There were subtle connections to contentious issues about which he and I periodically tangled.

The Waiting Room as Boundary and Bridge between Self-States and Unformulated Experience

Christine Kieffer

Christine Kieffer

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1413
Chicago, IL 60603-6258
E-mail: cckphd@aol.com

Christine Kieffer   Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; faculty. Departments of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Rush University Medical Center.

For both patient and analyst, the waiting room serves containing and expressive functions. The waiting room may serve as both a boundary between the analytic couple and a bridge to engagement. At times it can provide a means of titrating the intensity and duration of the affects activated by immersion in the analytic process: it can also serve to extend the boundaries of the analytic frame by providing a holding environment to facilitate metabolizing the impact of comings and goings. It also may be viewed as a membrane between self-states through which oscillating facets of dissociated or unformulated experience are enacted. Clinical material from the analysis of an adolescent girl illustrates these ideas.

Psychoanalytic Pluralities

When Theories Touch: A Historical and Theoretical Integration of Psychoanalytic Thought. by Steven J. Ellman. Cips Series on the Boundaries of Psychoanalysis. London: Karnac Books, 2010, 708 PP., $65.00.

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky

614 Watkins Street
Birmingham, MI 48009-1470
E-mail: dboesky@comcast.net

Dale Boesky  

Steven Ellman here offers an important and useful contribution to the literature of comparative psychoanalysis, and this book will be of value to the faculties and candidates of our institutes, as well as to psychoanalytic clinicians. Students of the historical development of psychoanalytic ideas will be indebted to him. Beginning with Freud, Ellman has undertaken the daunting task of summarizing and comparing the major ideas of a group of the eleven psychoanalytic pioneers who in his opinion have made the most important contributions to the advance of psychoanalysis, with special emphasis on the theory of object relations.

The book is divided into three sections: Freud (pp. 1-166); Major Post-Freudian Theorists (pp. 167-540); Contemporary Issues in Psychoanalysis (pp. 559-652.). One of Ellman's laudable goals is to demonstrate “that each position has a great deal to offer and can provide complementary viewpoints” (p. 414). He usefully argues against our chronic tendency to bifurcations and dichotomous thinking.

Ellman's pantheon includes Sigmund and Anna Freud, Heinz Hartmann, Melanie Klein (allotted the most space after Sigmund Freud), Fairbairn, Winnicott, Sullivan, Mahler, Kohut, Kernberg, and Bion. Section 2 concludes with chapter 16, by Arnold Richards and Arthur Lynch, whom Ellman has deputized to provide an historical overview of ego psychology and contemporary conflict theory. In two other chapters Ellman has invited others to collaborate with him: chapter 12, in which Anni Bergman joins him in an essay on Mahler, and chapter 14, in which Monica Karsky joins him in discussing the work of Kernberg.

I expect this book will serve as a widely used text and compendium for use in seminars on comparative psychoanalysis. But it is far more than that: the author has contributed thoughtful discussions about these contributors and expressed his own views on their various disagreements.

Investigating Merger: Subliminal Psychodynamic Activation and Oneness Motivation Research

Joel Weinberger and Brian Smith

Brian Smith

1 South Avenue
P.O. Box 701
Garden City, NY 11530-0701
E-mail: weinberger@adelphi.edu

Brian Smith   Doctoral student in clinical psychology, Demer Institute of Advanced Psychological Studies, Adelphi University.

Two programs of empirical research have endeavored to explicate some of the unconscious processes involved in adult phenomena of merger, symbiosis, or oneness. Starting in the 1960s under the leadership of Lloyd Silverman (see, e.g., Silverman, Lachmann, and Milich 1982), subliminal psychodynamic activation (SPA) has used subliminal methods to prime and manipulate these processes. Later research into what was termed oneness motivation (OM) posited the existence of a chronic personality variable and explored it independently of priming (see, e.g., Siegel and Weinberger 1998). These lines of research have established links between unconscious processes of oneness and a range of clinical and nonclinical outcomes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Dyad to Dialogue: Language and the Early Relationship in American Psychoanalytic Theory

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

122 South Michigan Avenue #1307
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz   Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Rush Medical College; faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

The centrality of language for a Freudian theory of mind and treatment has not been retained by most post-Freudian theorists. American writers have turned to academic developmental research on mother-child interactions to depict the preoedipal period as preverbal, presymbolic, non-conscious. This view presents the early relationship as developing in linear stages in which visual observational data (e.g., contingent behavior action patterns between two persons) are privileged over aural-oral data of communicational exchanges. An alternative view is presented that redefines the earliest relationship in terms of communicational exchanges, mediation, and dialogue. The claim is that understanding the nature of mediated communication keeps language central to psychoanalysis and reestablishes an intrapsychic dimension in the concept of relationship that is lost when relationship is reduced to behavioral patterns.

[[RunningHead]]

Thinking Outside the Box: A Metacognitive / Theory of Mind Perspective on Concrete Thinking

Richard Tuch

Richard Tuch

1800 Fairburn Avenue Suite 206
Los Angeles, CA 90025
E-mail: rtuch@aol.com

Richard Tuch  

Concrete thinking, an extraordinarily difficult condition to treat, has been psychoanalytically theorized to result from failures of symbolization—problems forming, linking, or fathoming the meaning of symbols—and/or failures of differentiation, resulting in difficulties distinguishing symbols from the thing being symbolized, fantasy from reality, self from other. Though difficulties symbolizing and differentiating are clearly evident in patients whose thinking is concrete, these may be a manifestation of concrete thinking rather than a root cause. Childhood thinking is characteristically concrete, and a persistence of such thinking into adulthood can be adequately explained as a failure to develop a more sophisticated theory of mind. Given that patients who exhibit such thinking tend to respond poorly to classic psychoanalytic interpretations, alternative technical approaches have been proposed. One such approach, “metacognitive” in nature, draws on a mode of thought used by gifted individuals that helps them “think outside the box” by dispensing with a typical pattern-recognition search so that novel meanings might be discovered. Metacognition, thoughts about one's thoughts and thought processes, facilitates symbolic thinking by creating sufficient distance from one's thoughts to allow the consideration of alternative meanings.

The Effect of Unrealizable Technical Ideals

Beth Steinberg

Beth Steinberg

3321 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
E-mail: besteinberg@comcast.net

Beth Steinberg  

In a packed room on Sunday morning, the last day of the San Francisco meeting, an eager crowd gathered to participate in a panel discussion on the question of how we view certain “technical ideals” in light of our evolving understanding of the mind and our ongoing attempt to articulate and refine our ideas about what psychoanalysis is and how we can best make use of it to help our patients.

Mary Margaret McClure, the chair, explained that this panel was originally intended to discuss Freud's technical recommendations of “anonymity, neutrality, and abstinence” and the difficulties that such ideals pose in the current climate of intense debate about the ideals of psychoanalysis, as well as the reality of the challenges we face in contemporary clinical practice. For example, what does it mean to have to live with, let alone live up to, technical ideals that are impossible to live up to, that are unrealizable? What kind of toll does it take on the psychoanalyst, and on the field as a whole, to embrace an ideal that cannot realistically be achieved?

Since it is no longer clear that these technical concepts are the ideals of psychoanalysis in the way they once were, this panel took up the issues of use of the couch, frequency of sessions, and free association. How do we hold these technical recommendations around the frame, and what do they mean in light of our our evolving understanding? Do they define what psychoanalysis is? If we feel that they do, are we in danger not only of being rigid, but of collapsing a larger understanding of what constitutes a psychoanalytic process and how we use it to the benefit of our patients? El vacío mental [The Mental Void]. By Jaime Lutenberg. Lima: Siklos, 2007, 263 pp.

Gabriela Legorreta

Gabriela Legorreta

603 Roslyn Avenue
Westmount H3Y 2VI
Quebec
CANADA
E-mail: gabriela@motion.psych.mcgill.ca

Gabriela Legorreta  

Jaime Lutenberg's, creative, intriguing, and rigorous work is clearly evident in his new book, El vacío mental (The Mental Void), which opens up new avenues of inquiry into what are referred to these days as the “new pathologies.” His conceptualizations carry with them new theoretical understanding and new clinical approaches to psychoanalytic work with these patients.

The creativity of Lutenberg's thinking is reflected in his ability to combine familiar psychoanalytic concepts in a novel fashion and his ability to introduce fresh ideas. Based solidly in Freudian metapsychology, Lutenberg, a training analyst of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association, has also been influenced by an eclectic mix of authors from around the world, including Wilfred Bion, José Bleger, André Green, Melanie Klein, David Liberman, Margaret Mahler, E. Pichon-Rivière, Harold Searles, Frances Tustin, and D. W. Winnicott.

Inspired by the work of David Liberman (1976) on psychoanalysis and communication, and by his passion for music, Lutenberg's inquiry begins by identifying different types of silences. Lutenberg brings to the associative sequences of patients a distinct way of listening. Registering not just the alternations between silences and the discourse proper, he makes a distinction regarding the quality of silences. Some point to the presence of repressed material; others indicate a void. This distinction is the starting point for his inquiry.

The “empty silence,” characterized by its hollow quality, is the signpost of the mental void. It is a silence emerging from a split-off segment of the psyche. It is, so to speak, a silent silence. This empty silence can be registered only through the analyst's countertransference. The analyst can always recognize the silence that signals a mental void through his or her own experience.

Lutenberg allows us to witness the clinical experiences with these challenging patients that led him to develop the concept of the mental void. Throughout, one can appreciate his ability to move back and forth between the clinical and the theoretical.

Bennett Simon

Bennett Simon

44B Burroughs Street
Jamaica Plain, MA 02130
E-mail: bennett_simon@hms.harvard.edu

Bennett Simon  

This is a book that deals with perversions—a subject on which everyone has an opinion and every opinion raises more questions that it answers. Consider the word, perversion. The term has fallen out of professional fashion, and a succession of alternatives have been proposed. What do we call “them”—the practices? Paraphilias, neosexualities? De gustibus or dis-gustibus? What's in a name? What do we call “them,” the people who perform these practices? What do we feel about them? And are “they” all alike? Is a young man arousing himself by putting on Victoria's Secret bra and panties in any way psychodynamically related to a priest molesting young boys or girls, let alone a rapist or sadistic torturer? And if the answer is yes, then what about the massive differences in behavior and kinds of harm to others? If no, then how can the same word be used to describe both?

“You Ought To!”: A Psychoanalytic Study of the Superego and Conscience. By Bernard Barnett. London: Karnac Books, 2007, xviii + 174 pp., $32.95 paperback.

Lawrence Levenson

Lawrence Levenson

240 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: lawrence.levenson@yale.edu

Lawrence Levenson  

Do psychoanalysts still care about psychic structure? It's a fair question to ask at a time when analysts increasingly are drawn to being “near”—near to the here-and-now process, near to transference and countertransference, near to enactments and to the meeting of two minds. Analysts today are much more likely to speak of the self and subjectivity than of ego and superego. In today's world, the idea of psychic structures may strike analysts as distant instead of near, old instead of “now.”

In “You Ought To!”: A Psychoanalytic Study of the Superego and Conscience, the British psychoanalyst Bernard Barnett makes a valuable contribution to contemporary analytic discourse with this thoughtful study of the superego, one of the two great structures of the mind (the other being the ego) engaged in managing an individual's instinctual life. Readers of Barnett's slender but rich volume will be reminded of the remarkable developmental story of the superego in which early, important external relationships are transmuted into an enduring internal structure. Fueling this transformation during childhood are primal anxieties that, incurring parental disapproval, will have traumatic consequences, either the loss of parental love or bodily injury such as castration. When those fears come to a head with the oedipus complex, the superego becomes firmly established (it is, famously, “heir to the oedipus complex”) to enforce good behavior by the child. In essence, by internalizing—and structuralizing—parental injunctions the child chooses parental love and protection over gratification of desires.

If love of the parents during childhood launches the formation of the superego, “murder” of the parents spurs its consolidation during adolescence. Both Loewald and Winnicott refer to the emancipatory murder of the parents that is necessary for an individual to develop a superego independent of parental authority. For Loewald (1980), it is not “murder” (in quotes) but an actual form of murder. “[It] is no exaggeration to say,” Loewald writes, “that the assumption of responsibility for one's own life and its conduct is in psychic reality tantamount to the murder of the parents…. In an important sense, by evolving our own autonomy, our own superego, and by engaging in non-incestuous object relations, we are killing our parents” (p. 389). Winnicott (1971)

Psychoanalytic Training: The Lacanian Tradition

The Trainings of the Psychoanalyst. By Annie Tardits. Translated by Marc du Ry. London: Karnac Books, 2010, xii + 164 pp., $34.95 paperback.

Calvern Narcisi

Calvern Narcisi

4900 Cherry Creek South Drive, Suite 10
Denver, CO 80246
E-mail: cnarcisi@aol.com

Calvern Narcisi  

The Trainings of the Psychoanalyst was an extremely challenging book to review. Its author, Annie Tardits, a French Lacanian psychoanalyst, first published the book in French in 2000. The English edition, published by Karnac Books last year, was translated by Marc du Ry, a London psychoanalyst connected to The Centre for Freudian Analysis and Research Library (CFAR). This group has among its goals the propagation of Lacanian psychoanalysis in the UK, as well as the translation of classic Lacanian texts into English.

At 164 pages, I expected this to be a fairly quick and easy read, even for someone not well versed in Lacanian theory. But either the author's writing style or the translator's has managed to produce a text as enigmatic and at times impenetrable as Lacan's lectures. In addition, the book is rife with grammatical, syntactical, and metaphorical errors. Despite all this, Tardits's conviction regarding the benefits of Lacan's training model compelled me to read and reread the text, as well as to consult other sources on Lacanian theory and training. If the author's intent was to stimulate further in-depth inquiry into Lacan's training model, she has succeeded. If, however, her intent was to write an unbiased and comprehensive text describing Lacan's thinking regarding the creation of a psychoanalyst, she has not. In the very brief introduction, Tardits focuses on Lacan's distinction between psychoanalytic training and the training of an individual analyst. Also in the introduction, Lacan's controversial notion of the “pass” makes its first appearance. We will return to this in detail later.

The first of the book's six chapters is titled “The First Analysts.” Under the subhead “A Testimony,” Tardits writes about Lou Andreas-Salomé, a layperson who without formal training became an analyst.

The Relational Tradition: Landscape and Canon

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: adrienneeharris@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

This essay charts the origins, influences, and evolution of the relational tradition in contemporary psychoanalysis. Considering the theoretical and philosophical influences from nineteenth-century Americans like William James and C. S Pierce, and noting the seminal modern work of Steven Mitchell and Jay Greenberg in opening a critique of one-person focused drive theory, the essay follows developments over a quarter century. Hallmarks of the relational approach—social construction, two-person psychologies, multiple self-states, social regulation and construction of identities like gender and sexual orientation, and an evolving theory of clinical practice—are reviewed. New developments in clinical theory, in the study of identity categories, in the work on embodiment and enactment, and in developmental models are also reviewed.

The Analyst's Words and Wording: Do They Still Matter?

Carol Levin

Carol Levin

2147 Commons Parkway
Okemos, MI 48864-3987
E-mail: levinc@msu.edu

Carol Levin  

In opening this clinical and theoretical panel, organized to explore not just if words and wording still matter to psychoanalysts but also how they matter, Joseph Lichtenberg reminded panel and audience that many analysts today no longer consider language-based communication leading to interpretation and insight to be the principal agent of therapeutic change. We are working in an era in which the pendulum in analytic theorizing on therapeutic action has swung from a focus on verbalization to a focus on implicit, often ineffable, nonverbal processes like relational interconnectedness, the container function, moments of meeting, disciplined spontaneous engagements, empathic immersion, the resolution of enactments and dissociation, the repair of ruptures, and the role of “the third” as variously theorized. Perhaps, Lichtenberg thought, not enough is being said these days about how the words and wording that we choose in speaking to our patients contribute to the therapeutic action of analytic process.

Lichtenberg articulated the vast terrain encompassed by his question “Do the analyst's words and wording still matter?” by posing a series of rhetorical questions designed to explore where we are as a field today. Are some word choices better than others? Are analysts' words theory-based? Do analysts know this is so? How much importance does an analyst or his or her theory place on the mutative effects of spontaneity and affective engagement as against carefully reasoned explanations? How much of word and phrasing choice is determined by individual, culturally influenced communication style regardless of an analyst's theory and training? Are an analyst's words and wording choices co-created bidirectionally (sometimes or all the time) in each dyad and each moment? Is it possible to know if one verbal approach, for example

This paper considers the issue of systematic empirical research versus clinical case studies raised by Hoffman (2009). A rebuttal of Hoffman's arguments is offered, followed by an argument that each method addresses itself to different questions and that posing them in opposition is not fruitful. Finally, criteria and requirements of the case study method are proposed that, if met, would enhance its evidential value.

Chestnut Lodge and the Psychoanalytic Approach to Psychosis

John Kafka

John Kafka

7834 Aberdeen Road
Bethesda, MD 20814
E-mail: jkafka@tidalwave.net

John Kafka   Training and Supervising Analyst, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute; Professor Emeritus, Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, George Washington University Medical School.

The study of psychosis has a long history in psychoanalysis, as does the debate over the suitability of psychoanalysis for treating schizophrenia. For decades, Chestnut Lodge was not only a hospital but also a clinical research and educational institution. A unique patient-staff ratio—about twenty analytic therapists for a hundred patients—made possible prolonged and intense clinical work with schizophrenic and other severely disturbed patients. Interstaff discussions were encouraged and facilitated. This quasi-academic approach to in-depth individual case studies led to clinical findings and theoretical formulations that had a significant impact on developments in psychoanalysis, both here and abroad. Many of these findings and theoretical formulations are relevant to current studies and treatments of psychotic and nonpsychotic patients.

Aaron Esman  

The relation between psychoanalysis and Surrealism, a movement that played a dominant role in art and culture in the Western world for at least a generation between the wars, is an episode in the history of psychoanalysis that has been of greater interest to art historians than to psychoanalysts. A very few references appear in the psychoanalytic literature (e.g., Kaplan 1989; Davis 1973), while the field of art history is replete with articles, books, and passing observations; Freud's name is scattered through them like autumn leaves on the grass. In his classic History of Surrealist Painting, for instance, Marcel Jean (1959) cites Freud five times, while Peter Webb (1985), in his book on the multimedia artist Hans Bellmer, refers to Freud on eleven occasions. Polizzotti's biography of André Breton (1995) has over thirty such references, and Mundy (2002), in Surrealism: Desire Unbound, refers to him twenty times, cites eighteen of his writings, and provides a listing of French translations of his works from 1896 to 1969.

The Surrealists' romance with Freud and psychoanalysis began with the litterateur André Breton, who, at a time of enormous literary and cultural ferment in Europe (exemplified by the iconoclastic Dada movement founded in Zurich in 1917), worked during his youthful service in World War I as a psychiatric aide in a military hospital. Breton had long been partial to German literature and philosophy, and under the tutelage of one Dr. Leroy, a psychiatrist with whom he worked, discovered and became fascinated by Freud's writings, particularly The Interpretation of Dreams.

[[RunningHead]]

Charles Brenner: A Practitioner's Theorist

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129-B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021

Lawrence Friedman  

To avoid certain errors in practice, Charles Brenner offered an holistic substitute for the Freudian structural model of the mind. He used the term compromise formation ambiguously to refer to both actions and states, so as to render unnecessary what he considered artificial, judgmental attitudes embodied in images of psychic structures. He believed that a theory of conflicting structures transforms the phenomenological drama of the patient's actual life-world into an artificial drama of contending intrapsychic parties that may reflect the analyst's values. According to Brenner, the meaning of life, with its desires, fears, and regrets, is structured forever in the first articulation of the family drama, and that is all the structure a practitioner should have in mind. In principle, the ambiguity of the term compromise formation allows for observed continuities in human life, and might have inspired an ambitious theoretician to exploit that option for an account of character, but that aspect of theory moves in a direction opposite to Brenner's practical mission. For the same practical reason Brenner refused to acknowledge gradations of mental operation, such as differences in maturity, or style or level of thinking, so the theory cannot say how change can take place, analytic or otherwise. These lacunae in theory were unblinkingly (if implicitly) accepted in pursuit of Brenner's goal, which was not to polish up theory but to cleanse the analyst's mind of concepts that subtly interfere with the essential nondirectiveness of treatment. His theoretical minimalism and exclusive concern with practical consequences can be recognized as a peculiarly North American attitude to psychoanalysis.

Freud's technical papers, “The Dynamics of Transference” in particular, established most of the basis for a century of clinical psychoanalytic work. A contemporary reading of that paper illustrates both the power of the original presentation and how far we have come. Close readings are given of crucial passages, including retranslations where Strachey's English arguably distorts Freud's language or intent. More broadly, Freud's conclusions are examined to reveal correspondences with current thinking or its foreshadowing. Although Freud drove toward unifying conclusions, he at the same time described a disparate array of clinical phenomena. The paper's central points are situated historically, both in Freud's evolution and in the subsequent evolution toward the pluralistic clinical theory of today. Freud encountered many of the challenges we face, noting them in ways both clinically useful and fruitful for other theorists. The use of the paper's central tenets is traced historically, showing how ideas that provide a radical, liberating, and facilitating guide for analysts can over time devolve into hackneyed rules. Finally, it is noted how transference phenomena are increasingly being discussed as a specific type of dyadic human experience. J. David Miller   Training and Supervising Analyst, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, George Washington University Medical School, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences.

This study of Giorgio Morandi, the great twentieth-century Italian painter, departs from the model of Freud's essay on Leonardo, in which he creates a psychodynamic hypothesis by linking the art with facts and conjecture about the artist's life. Because personal information about Morandi is remarkably scarce, we can make few connections between his art and his individual psychology. But Morandi's works can inform psychoanalysis and enhance analytic listening when we reflect on the paintings themselves, our responses to them, and the responses of art critics. This approach to his art evokes core principles of clinical theory, demonstrates the overlap between the creative process in art and analysis, and exemplifies the value of interdisciplinary study for analytic education.

Pretend Play and Development in Early Childhood (with Implications for the Oedipal Phase)

Karen Gilmore

Karen Gilmore

305 East 18th Street
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: kjg23@columbia.edu

Karen Gilmore   Training and Supervising Analyst and Director of the Child Division, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Pretend play reflects both the unique mental organization and the developmental challenges of early to middle childhood, with a trajectory that parallels the monumental transformation of the toddler to the school-age child. Despite evidence for various forms of playing throughout the life cycle, the flowering of symbolic play in this phase is specific, essential, and typically transient in terms of its dominance in the life of the child. It reflects the simultaneous emergence, processing, and integration of the remarkable developmental advances occurring during this period, most especially the capacity to symbolize, in the service of the exponentially expanded psychosexual/social/emotional force field implied in the contemporary use of the term oedipus complex. Moreover, it constitutes a particular mental organization and ego state, with idiosyncratic mentation, affect regulation, and relationship to inner and outer experience, that accompanies the child's gradual orientation to consensual reality. Subsequent access to some form of this state varies widely among individuals but is rarely fully comparable.

Agency as a Psychoanalytic Idea

Joseph Caston

Joseph Caston

2710 Broderick Street
San Francisco, CA 94123
E-mail: joecaston@comcast.net

Joseph Caston   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

Competent agency is a basic assumption of psychoanalytic change. Yet as an aspect of health, personal agency has in the main been only intuitively addressed and remains clinically unsystematized. Here experience-near and observer-centered criteria are developed that assess a person-as-agent's competence in particular domains. These ideas, clinically illustrated, stand as an operational framework that helps thinking and talking about agency in everyday clinical events and psychoanalytic outcomes. Three specific criteria are proposed: reversibility, self-observation, and appropriateness. The first is descriptively polar; together the three apply to each given context of action. They can also serve to ground future research. In this regard, several empirical psychoanalytic studies of adults and children that exemplify measurable aspects of agency are reviewed. Once clinical markers of personal agency are articulated, it will not be necessary to resolve the free will debate: pragmatically, we need only put such distinctions to work.

Arnold Goldberg   Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Cynthia Oudejans Harris Professor of Psychiatry, Rush Medical College.

This paper expands upon and further develops the centrality of empathy in psychoanalysis as offered by Heinz Kohut. Using clinical examples, it differentiates sustained empathy as the distinctive component of psychoanalysis, and it demonstrates some of the difficulties in determining the boundaries of empathy in the practice of psychoanalysis. A further distinction is from mind reading, a purely cognitive exercise, as is intuition (Carruthers 2009). To pursue a psychoanalytic perception of empathy one must confront its limitations and go beyond the somewhat simplistic claim of its unquestioned therapeutic effect. Empathy is more than a cognitive act, and as sustained over time it can be difficult to achieve, can be misunderstood, and can at times have no therapeutic effect.

Anna Aragno

Anna Aragno

140-142 West End Avenue

Anna Aragno   Private practice, New York.

Against the backdrop of a broad survey of the literature on applied psychoanalysis, a number of concepts underpinning the metapsychology of art are revisited and revised: sublimation; interrelationships between primary and secondary processes; symbolization; “fantasy”; and “cathexis.” Concepts embedded in dichotomous or drive/energic contexts are examined and reformulated in terms of a continuum of semiotic processes. Freudian dream structure is viewed as a biological/natural template for nonrepressive artistic forms of sublimation. The synthesis presented proposes a model of continuous rather than discontinuous processes, in a nonenergic, biosemiotic metatheoretical framework.

“The Noise Monitor”: A Developmental Perspective on Verbal and Nonverbal Meaning-Making in Psychoanalysis

Alexandra Harrison and Ed Tronick

Ed Tronick

183 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: aharrisonmd@comcast.net

Ed Tronick   University Distinguished Professor, Department of Psychology, University of Massachusetts Boston; Chief Faculty, University of Massachusetts Boston Infant-Parent Postgraduate Certificate Program; Chief Faculty, Department of Newborn Medicine, Harvard Medical School.

This paper discusses a contribution of developmental theory to the psychoanalytic concept of “the talking cure.” The developmental theory presented is the dyadic expansion of consciousness model (Tronick 2007), a model consistent with the principles of nonlinear systems theory. The concept of “polysemic bundles” as a way of understanding the multiple simultaneous meaning-making processes occurring in dyadic communication is introduced. The theoretical discussion—focused primarily on the analysis of children—is illustrated with descriptions of videotaped sequences from the first session in the analysis of a five-year-old boy. The relevance of these insights to the analytic treatment of adults is then considered.

3667 Sacramento Street

Stephen Seligman  

From its beginnings, psychoanalysis has located itself in relation to the broadest array of sources, ranging from the fine arts, through politics, culture, and history, to the sciences. Freud was explicit about the value of consilience with other fields, especially the natural and physical sciences. However, in the century since its inception, analysis has become less certain about its links to adjacent fields. Shifts in scientific paradigms, especially in physics and neurobiology, have disproven some of the assumptions on which the original Freudian metapsychology was based. Meanwhile, analysis has established itself as an independent, largely clinical discipline, and philosophical and political currents, from hermeneutics and feminism, for example, have dislocated some of the original methodological certainties. Over the years, updates have been offered, with varying success.

Daniel Stern has been in the forefront of this project. Most psychoanalysts think of him as a leading developmentalist, rather than as a cutting-edge metapsychologist. Taken as a whole, Stern's work proposes a flexible, clinically resonant analytic model of mental life rooted in direct contact with lived experience, based in the actual body, emotions, and what is happening moment to moment both within and between minds. This all leads to lively meaning-making and meaning-processing, integrating life processes from basic levels as small as cells and neurotransmitters, through intermediate processes such as movement and emotions, to collective social and cultural forms such as language and the arts. This project draws directly on the contemporary scientific disciplines closest to psychoanalysis: neuroscience, developmental and cognitive psychology, philosophy, and the arts.

Few psychoanalysts have taken on such a broad range in regard to what they have sought to theorize. Not surprisingly, Freud was the most ambitious. Two developmentalists tackling broader questions, Erikson and Winnicott, have been among the handful of his most eloquent successors in this regard. Developmental thinking may indeed lend itself to application to our basic questions, since it looks at whole persons and also at very young ones, who are often thought of as offering clues to the question of what is most fundamental in human motivation and organization.

The declaredly atheoretical DSM-III (and its successors), the diagnostic manual of the American Psychiatric Association, was created to enhance diagnostic reliability for research, epidemiological survey, and governmental and insurance categorization and reimbursement purposes. It has, however, exhibited many inadequacies for psychodynamic diagnosis and case formulation for treatment planning and outcome assessment, and its claimed diagnostic reliability has turned out to be less than originally projected. The psychoanalytically sponsored Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual (PDM) marks a return to a theoretically (psychoanalytically) based diagnostic frame and was created as a supplement to, or replacement for, DSM (depending on the precise clinical need), for use in psychodynamic diagnosis and treatment planning.

Shelley Orgel  

This volume of essays attests the continuing work of André Green, who contributed its keynote chapter, “The Construction of the Lost Father.” The book's title, The Dead Father, references Green's classic essay, “The Dead Mother” (1983), whose clinical and theoretical explorations of maternal depression and the joined role of the father as the third have been widely appreciated as essential contributions to an ever more complex psychoanalytic understanding of development.

The origins and purposes of the symposium whose participants created this volume are explained by Lila Kalinich in the first of two introductory essays (the second is by Stuart W. Taylor). Kalinich writes: “Our awakened awareness of the terrorist threat after 9/11 meant that … the enemy's terms of engagement seemed no longer intelligible or predictable. … Even the dress code of the women becomes a front in the clash between Western civilization and fervent Islamism. … The sexual body is again a flash point” (p. 2). It was in 2001, Kalinich concludes, that “we were forced to question the character, the existence of the Father as provider of rules, intelligible meanings, a referee at the game” (p. 2). We now must try “to determine whether our models of individual psychology could shed any light” (p. 2) on the transformations in American culture and in the rest of the world as well.

To undertake such an effort, in 2006 a group of analysts from the Association for Psychoanalytic Medicine joined with analysts and scholars from around the world in an international symposium to take a fresh look at the functions of the Father and his representations in the psyche. The editors deserve much credit for their dedicated efforts to assemble a coherently structured volume of fascinating linked essays from this wealth of contributions. Most authors regarded the killing of the Father as imagined in Freud's Totem and Taboo (1912-1913) and Moses and Monotheism (1939) as the origin of a universal imperative to represent or symbolize the Father.
