In recent years psychoanalytically oriented researchers have invested in outcome studies to provide empirical evidence for the effectiveness of their treatments. This has led to a considerable (and still growing) body of research about the effectiveness of long-term ambulatory psychoanalytic treatment (Leichsenring and Rabung 2008; Shedler 2010). The present study investigated whether long-term psychoanalytic treatment (psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy) is effective in reducing symptomatic distress and improving personality functioning.

Method

The total sample consisted of 231 participants from four mental health care organizations (Nederlands Psychoanalytisch Instituut, De Gelderse Roos, Mediant, Parnassia/Psy-Q) with 93 persons in psychoanalysis (PA) and 138 persons in psychoanalytic psychotherapy (PP). In this study we used a quasi-experimental, cross-sectional design with four cohorts differing in phase of treatment (pre, during, post, follow-up) in order to obtain information about the effects of long-term treatments within a relatively short period of time. The concept of transference and the therapeutic benefit of insight are central postulates of the psychoanalytic method. In a recent Journal of Counseling and Clinical Psychology article, Johansson et al. tested the hypothesis that insight is the mechanism behind the long-term effects of transference interpretation. The authors argued that focusing on transference issues between patient and therapist leads to an increased capacity to reflect on and understand interpersonal problems, which in turn leads to an improvement in interpersonal functioning and treatment outcome. The authors further argued that patients with well-developed interpersonal skills and insight into social relations might benefit more from this approach than patients who are less developed in these areas of functioning.

To test this hypothesis, the authors conducted a dismantling, randomized controlled trial among 122 patients with depressive disorders, anxiety disorders, personality disorders, and interpersonal problems. Patients were randomly assigned to a year of weekly psychodynamic therapy with either moderate levels of transference interpretation or no transference interpretation. Based on above or below average scores on the Quality of Object Relations scale, patients were considered to have either high or low pretreatment interpersonal skills, respectively. Change in interpersonal functioning was assessed with the Psychodynamic Functioning Scale at pre-treatment, post-treatment, one-year, and three-year follow-up intervals.

Mark Gehrie

Mark Gehrie

405 North Wabash Avenue, Unit 4505

Mark Gehrie   Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

The concept of the selfobject was central to Heinz Kohut's psychology of the self. With an eye to studying the development of narcissism and its implications for the growth of new psychic structure, this concept is reviewed and reassessed. Post-Kohutian complexities regarding its definition and use extend our consideration of the development of narcissism beyond archaic configurations toward further evolution of the self and the nature of mature narcissism. The hypothesis is offered that developing narcissism and the growth of self-regulation impact the acquisition of new structure and new capacities through the emergence of newly potentiated aspects of the self. The implications of these emergent qualities of the self are examined in the context of our understanding of self-esteem regulation, the state of the self, and the goals of psychoanalysis. A clinical example illustrates how technique and process in an analysis may be organized around the development of such new capacities.

Harold Blum   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Training Analyst, NYU School of Medicine; Executive Director, Sigmund Freud Archives.

My tenure as the second editor of Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association followed the remarkable twenty years of John Frosch's founding editorship. With the support of his polymath associate editor, Nathaniel Ross, and a distinguished editorial board, an outstanding journal was created at the forefront of psychoanalytic discourse. I had a tough act to follow.

I will comment on my experience as editor, some changes in the journal itself, and some of the significant trends in psychoanalysis evident during that time. Of course, writing from a contemporary perspective provides the advantages of distance and hindsight, but it also introduces the concern of historical distortion. I'm of necessity being selective, and inevitably subjective. I will not be able to address many of the important issues or cite all of the many authors who during my tenure made seminal contributions to psychoanalysis.

Youthful at the time by psychoanalytic standards, I experienced my appointment as a great honor and privilege, as well as an awesome responsibility. The editorship provided endless intellectual stimulation and challenge, as well as an excess of highly disciplined work. A main task on becoming editor was to maintain a balance between psychoanalytic preservation and psychoanalytic innovation. I was initially concerned with maintaining the autonomy of the journal and of the editorial process against the political and personal pressures, perhaps inevitable, that confront any journal, especially one sponsored by a professional association. I was pleased that during my tenure the editorial process and the journal remained entirely independent.

A first, perhaps superficial change in JAPA was a redesign of the journal. Among the more significant changes was the appointment for the first time of an assistant editor for child and adolescent analysis and child development.

Anne Erreich   Training and Supervising Analyst, The Institute for Psychoanalytic Education Affiliated with New York University School of Medicine; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, NYU Medical Center.

The concepts of oedipal guilt, survivor guilt, and separation guilt are examined using clinical material from a child case to demonstrate the intermingling of these constructs. A brief review of their evolution in the psychoanalytic literature reveals a frequent conflation of the terms guilt and fear, the former at times standing in for both meanings. The fear/guilt distinction and the subsequent differentiation of guilt into oedipal, survivor, and separation guilt have implications for how analysts understand and interpret particular kinds of clinical material. Two sets of adult clinical data are next presented: the first illustrates a shift from interpreting a patient's fear of retribution for forbidden desires to interpreting guilt over pursuing those desires. The second vignette illustrates a common dynamic in which a patient's fear/anxiety regarding the ability to lead an independent life defends against deeper feelings of guilt over this same desire. This latter dynamic can play an important role in negative therapeutic reactions and interminable analyses. Developmental research suggests that toward the end of the first year of life, infants' capacity to attribute independent mental states and intentionality to self and others allows for the rudimentary experience of guilt.
